metadata.title: ORDOS Problems
metadata.description: Crowdsourced open problems in Operations Research, Operations Management, and Management Science (with related market design and online algorithms). Modeled on erdosproblems.com. Each problem has a short statement and a complete, model-level statement suitable for handing to a solver.

problems.title: Matroid Secretary Conjecture
problems.short_statement: Weighted elements of a matroid arrive in uniformly random order and must be irrevocably accepted or rejected while keeping the accepted set independent. Does some algorithm always capture at least a constant fraction of the maximum-weight independent set, for every matroid?
problems.open_problem: Determine whether such a constant-competitive algorithm exists.
problems.full_statement: Let $M=(E,\mathcal{I})$ be a matroid on ground set $E$ with independent sets $\mathcal{I}$, equipped with weights $w:E\to\mathbb{R}_{\ge 0}$. The elements of $E$ are presented to an online algorithm one at a time in uniformly random order. Upon each arrival, the algorithm must irrevocably accept or reject the element, subject to the constraint that the set of accepted elements always remains in $\mathcal{I}$ (independent in the matroid). Let $\mathrm{OPT}$ denote the maximum-weight independent set of $M$, and let $\mathrm{ALG}$ denote the set the algorithm accepts. The Matroid Secretary Conjecture asks: does there exist a universal constant $c$ and an online algorithm such that $\mathbb{E}[w(\mathrm{ALG})]\ge \frac{1}{c}\,w(\mathrm{OPT})$ for every matroid $M$ and every weighting $w$? The strong form of the conjecture posits that the optimal constant is $c\to e$. Determine whether such a constant-competitive algorithm exists.
problems.known_results: The best known guarantee for arbitrary matroids is $O(\log\log \operatorname{rank})$-competitive, due independently to Lachish and to Feldman, Svensson, and Zenklusen, and this general bound has not improved since 2014. Constant-competitive algorithms are known for many important matroid classes, including graphic, transversal, laminar, and regular matroids, and for several relaxed arrival models. The central open question is whether all matroids admit a universal constant-competitive algorithm; the strongest hoped-for form would match the classical secretary constant $e$.

problems.title: Optimal ratio for the free-order (order-selection) prophet inequality
problems.short_statement: A gambler facing $n$ items with known distributions may choose the order in which to inspect them, then observes values online and stops irrevocably, keeping one. What is the exact optimal competitive ratio against the expected maximum in this free-order (order-selection) model?
problems.open_problem: Determine the exact value of $\alpha^*_{\mathrm{OS}}$.
problems.full_statement: There are $n$ items with values drawn independently from known distributions $D_1,\dots,D_n$. In the free-order (order-selection) prophet inequality, the gambler first selects an inspection order $\sigma$ (a permutation committing to the sequence in which items will be observed), then observes the realized values online one at a time and applies a stopping rule, irrevocably keeping exactly one value. The benchmark is the prophet's expected maximum $\mathbb{E}[\max_i X_i]$. Define the optimal order-selection ratio $$\alpha^*_{\mathrm{OS}} = \sup_{(\sigma,\text{stop})}\inf_{\{D_i\}} \frac{\mathbb{E}[X_{\text{alg}}]}{\mathbb{E}[\max_i X_i]},$$ where the supremum is over all order-and-stopping-rule strategies and the infimum is over all instances. It is known that order selection achieves $\ge 0.7258$, while no strategy in the random-order model exceeds $0.7254$, so order selection strictly beats random order. Determine the exact value of $\alpha^*_{\mathrm{OS}}$.
problems.known_results: Order-selection guarantee 0.7258 and random-order hardness 0.7254 (Bubna-Chiplunkar, EC 2023), establishing a strict separation. Essentially settled to about 0.7258 but no exact tight constant proven, so technically open.

problems.title: Single-sample prophet inequality for general matroids
problems.short_statement: In the single-sample model the algorithm does not know the distributions but sees exactly one prior sample from each random variable before the online sequence. Does a single sample yield a constant-fraction-of-optimum guarantee for every matroid?
problems.open_problem: Determine whether a single sample suffices to obtain a constant-competitive guarantee for every matroid.
problems.full_statement: Let $M=(E,\mathcal{I})$ be a matroid on ground set $E$ with independent sets $\mathcal{I}$. Each element $e\in E$ has a value $X_e\sim D_e$ drawn independently, but the distributions $D_e$ are unknown to the algorithm. In the single-sample model, before the online phase the algorithm observes exactly one sample $\tilde X_e\sim D_e$ for each element $e$. Then the actual values $X_e$ arrive online, and the algorithm irrevocably builds an independent set (the accepted set must remain in $\mathcal{I}$). The question is whether there exists a universal constant $c$ such that $\mathbb{E}[w(\mathrm{ALG})]\ge \frac{1}{c}\,\mathbb{E}[\max_{S\in\mathcal{I}} w(S)]$ using a single sample per element, for every matroid, where $w(\mathrm{ALG})$ is the total value of the accepted independent set. For the single-choice case (rank-1 / uniform matroid of rank 1) the tight answer is known to be $1/2$. Determine whether a single sample suffices to obtain a constant-competitive guarantee for every matroid.
problems.known_results: Single-choice single-sample is tight at $1/2$ (Rubinstein-Wang-Weinberg 2020). For general matroids the best is ($1/4$ - $\epsilon$)-competitive using $O(\operatorname{polylog} n)$ samples (EC 2024); whether a single sample gives a constant for general matroids is open and tied to the matroid secretary conjecture.

problems.title: Competitive ratio for online bipartite matching with general vertex arrivals
problems.short_statement: In the general-vertex-arrival model, vertices on both sides of a bipartite graph arrive online (each revealing its already-arrived neighbors) and must be matched irrevocably. What is the optimal competitive ratio for maximum cardinality matching?
problems.open_problem: Determine the optimal competitive ratio $\rho$ for maximum cardinality matching in this model.
problems.full_statement: Consider a bipartite graph in which all vertices (on both sides) arrive online in adversarial order. Each arriving vertex reveals its edges to previously arrived vertices, and must be matched irrevocably either upon arrival or at the moment it can no longer be matched. The goal is to maximize the cardinality of the matching, compared against the maximum cardinality matching of the full graph. This is the general-vertex-arrival model, which strictly generalizes the classical Karp-Vazirani-Vazirani one-sided arrival model (where offline vertices are fixed and only online vertices arrive, for which the optimal ratio is $1-1/e$). Determine the optimal competitive ratio $\rho$ for maximum cardinality matching in this model. Known: the best integral algorithm achieves $\tfrac12 + \Omega(1)$, the best fractional algorithm achieves $0.526$, and no algorithm exceeds $0.583$.
problems.known_results: Gamlath, Kapralov, Maggiori, Svensson, and Wajc gave the first integral algorithm beating the trivial $1/2$ barrier by a constant amount. Wang and Wong obtained a $0.526$ fractional guarantee, while the best known upper bound rules out ratios above $0.583$. This leaves a substantial gap, especially compared with the edge-arrival model, where the optimal ratio is essentially understood at $1/2$. The open problem is to determine the optimal competitive ratio for general vertex arrivals, or at least to close the interval between the current lower and upper bounds.

problems.title: Competitive ratio for fully online matching
problems.short_statement: In fully online matching, all vertices of a (possibly non-bipartite) graph arrive online with deadlines, and an unmatched vertex must be matched or dropped by its deadline. What is the optimal competitive ratio?
problems.open_problem: Determine the optimal competitive ratio.
problems.full_statement: In the fully online matching model, the vertices of a (possibly non-bipartite) graph arrive online one at a time. Each vertex has a deadline that occurs after all of its neighbors have arrived. An algorithm may match a vertex to any currently available neighbor at any time up to its deadline, and all matching decisions are irrevocable; at its deadline, an unmatched vertex must be either matched or permanently dropped. This generalizes the classical online bipartite matching model by allowing vertices on both sides (and non-bipartite graphs) to arrive online with deadlines. The benchmark is the maximum matching of the full graph. Determine the optimal competitive ratio. For bipartite graphs, the best integral algorithm is $0.569$-competitive, the best fractional algorithm is $0.6$-competitive, and no algorithm exceeds $0.613$.
problems.known_results: For bipartite fully online matching, the best integral algorithm currently achieves ratio $0.569$, due to Huang, Tang, Wu, and Zhang. Tang and Zhang improved the fractional guarantee to $0.6$, while Eckl and coauthors proved an impossibility result at $0.613$. The known upper and lower bounds are close enough to suggest a sharp constant may be accessible, but the exact competitive ratio is still open.

problems.title: Optimal ratio for vertex-weighted online matching in random order
problems.short_statement: For vertex-weighted online bipartite matching with weighted offline vertices and online vertices arriving in uniformly random order, one can beat the adversarial-optimal $1-1/e$. What is the exact best-possible competitive ratio?
problems.open_problem: Determine the optimal competitive ratio $\rho$ (the ratio of expected matched weight to the offline optimum) in the random-order vertex-weighted setting.
problems.full_statement: Consider online bipartite matching in which the offline vertices carry weights and the online vertices arrive in uniformly random order, each being matched irrevocably upon arrival to an available offline neighbor (or left unmatched). The objective is the total weight of matched offline vertices, compared against the offline optimum (maximum-weight matching). For adversarial one-sided arrivals the optimal competitive ratio for this vertex-weighted setting is exactly $1-1/e$; with uniformly random arrival order one can do strictly better. Determine the optimal competitive ratio $\rho$ (the ratio of expected matched weight to the offline optimum) in the random-order vertex-weighted setting. Known: $\rho \ge 0.662 > 1 - 1/e$, with no matching upper bound currently established.
problems.known_results: In the adversarial one-sided arrival model, the vertex-weighted problem is tight at $1-1/e$, by Aggarwal, Goel, Karande, and Mehta. Uniformly random arrival order makes the problem easier: algorithms of Huang, Tang, Wu, and Zhang and the later analysis of Jin and Williamson achieve ratio about $0.662$, strictly above $1-1/e$. No matching upper bound is known. The open problem is to identify the exact random-order competitive ratio and determine how much random order can improve over the adversarial threshold.

problems.title: Tight ratio for edge-weighted online matching with free disposal
problems.short_statement: In edge-weighted online bipartite matching with free disposal ('display ads'), each offline vertex holds one item but may discard a prior match for free if a better one arrives. What is the best-possible competitive ratio?
problems.open_problem: Determine the optimal competitive ratio $\rho$.
problems.full_statement: Consider edge-weighted online bipartite matching, motivated by display advertising. Online vertices arrive in adversarial order. Matching an online vertex $v$ to an offline vertex $u$ yields edge weight $w_{uv}$. Each offline vertex $u$ may hold at most one item, but is allowed free disposal: if a better-weight online vertex arrives, $u$ may discard its previously matched item at no cost and keep only its highest-weight match. The objective is to maximize the total weight retained, compared against the offline optimum. Greedy guarantees $1/2$; beating $1/2$ was a major open problem until 2020. Determine the optimal competitive ratio $\rho$. Known: $0.536 \le \rho \le 1 - 1/e$ in the integral case.
problems.known_results: The greedy algorithm is $1/2$-competitive. Fahrbach, Huang, Tao, and Zadimoghaddam were the first to beat the $1/2$ barrier, obtaining ratio $0.5086$ via online correlated selection, and Blanc and Charikar later improved the guarantee to $0.536$. On the impossibility side, no upper bound below $1-1/e$ is known. The open problem is to determine the tight competitive ratio for edge-weighted online matching with free disposal.

problems.title: AdWords with general bids: is 1-1/e achievable?
problems.short_statement: In AdWords, advertisers with budgets receive online queries and spend a bid (capped by remaining budget) per assignment. Without the small-bid assumption, is the optimal $1-1/e$ revenue ratio (or anything close) achievable?
problems.open_problem: Determine the optimal competitive ratio for general bids; in particular, is it $1-1/e$?
problems.full_statement: In the AdWords problem, each advertiser $i$ has a budget $B_i$, and queries $j$ arrive online. Assigning query $j$ to advertiser $i$ spends $\min(b_{ij}, \text{remaining } B_i)$, where $b_{ij}$ is the bid; in the general-bids setting these bids may be comparable to $B_i$ (i.e., there is no small-bid / tiny-bid assumption). Each query is assigned irrevocably upon arrival, and the goal is to maximize total spend (revenue). The optimal $1-1/e$ ratio of Mehta-Saberi-Vazirani-Vazirani holds only under the small-bid assumption (each bid tiny relative to the budget). Without it, greedy gives only $1/2$. Determine the optimal competitive ratio for general bids; in particular, is it $1-1/e$? Known: $0.5016 \le \rho \le 1 - 1/e$.
problems.known_results: When all bids are small relative to budgets, the MSVV algorithm gives the optimal $1-1/e$ guarantee. With arbitrary bid sizes, the simple greedy guarantee is only $1/2$, and Huang, Zhang, and Zhang were the first to beat this barrier, obtaining a ratio of $0.5016$ using panoramic online correlated selection. There is still no upper bound below $1-1/e$ for general bids. The open problem is to determine the true optimal competitive ratio in this large-bid regime, and in particular whether the small-bids constant $1-1/e$ can be recovered.

problems.title: The (deterministic) k-server conjecture
problems.short_statement: With $k$ mobile servers in a metric space serving requested points by moving a server to each request, is there for every metric space a deterministic online algorithm whose cost is at most $k$ times the optimal offline cost?
problems.open_problem: Determine whether the deterministic competitive ratio equals $k$ for every metric space.
problems.full_statement: In the $k$-server problem, an algorithm controls $k$ mobile servers located in a metric space $M$ and must serve a sequence of requested points: each request must be served by moving some server to the requested point, and the cost is the total distance moved by all servers. The deterministic $k$-server conjecture asks: for every metric space $M$ with $|M|\ge k+1$, does there exist a deterministic online $k$-server algorithm $A$ and a constant $c_M$ (depending only on $M$) such that $\mathrm{cost}_A(\sigma)\le k\cdot \mathrm{OPT}(\sigma)+c_M$ for all request sequences $\sigma$, where $\mathrm{OPT}(\sigma)$ is the optimal offline cost? Equivalently, is the deterministic competitive ratio exactly $k$? The matching lower bound of $k$ is already known, so the question is whether this lower bound is achievable. The best general upper bound is $2k-1$. Determine whether the deterministic competitive ratio equals $k$ for every metric space.
problems.known_results: The lower bound of $k$ is classical, so the conjecture asks whether this bound is always achievable. The best general deterministic upper bound is $2k-1$, obtained by Koutsoupias and Papadimitriou through the Work Function Algorithm. The conjectured ratio $k$ is known in several special cases, including $k=2$, line metrics, tree metrics, and metrics with only $k+1$ or $k+2$ points. The full metric-space case remains open.

problems.title: The right competitive ratio for randomized k-server (conjecture refuted)
problems.short_statement: The randomized $k$-server conjecture (an $O(\log k)$-competitive algorithm on every metric) was refuted in 2023 by an $\Omega(\log^2 k)$ lower bound. What is the true polylogarithmic dependence on $k$ (and the number of points $n$)?
problems.open_problem: Determine the optimal randomized competitive ratio as a function of $k$ (and $n$); in particular, is the truth $\Theta(\log^2 k)$?
problems.full_statement: Consider the $k$-server problem with a randomized online algorithm playing against an oblivious adversary, where the algorithm controls $k$ servers in a metric space, serves a sequence of requested points by moving servers, and pays total distance moved. The long-standing randomized $k$-server conjecture predicted that an $O(\log k)$-competitive randomized algorithm exists on every metric. This was refuted: Bubeck-Coester-Rabani proved an $\Omega(\log^2 k)$ lower bound on certain $(k+1)$-point metrics (with a universal $\Omega(\log k)$). The best upper bounds are $O(\log^2 k)$ on hierarchically separated trees (HSTs) and $\tilde O(\log^3 n\,\log^2 k)$ on general $n$-point metrics, where $n$ is the number of points. Determine the optimal randomized competitive ratio as a function of $k$ (and $n$); in particular, is the truth $\Theta(\log^2 k)$?
problems.known_results: Bubeck, Coester, and Rabani refuted the original $O(\log k)$ conjecture by proving an $\Omega(\log^2 k)$ lower bound on certain metrics, together with a universal $\Omega(\log k)$ lower bound. On the positive side, $O(\log^2 k)$ algorithms are known for hierarchically separated trees, and polylogarithmic guarantees are known for general metrics. The problem has therefore shifted from the old logarithmic conjecture to determining the correct polylogarithmic exponent.

problems.title: Additive integrality gap of the bin packing (Gilmore-Gomory) LP
problems.short_statement: For bin packing, how far can the integer optimum be from the value of the Gilmore-Gomory configuration LP relaxation in absolute (additive) terms? Is the gap always $O(1)$ extra bins, or can it grow?
problems.open_problem: Determine whether the additive integrality gap is $O(1)$ or $\Theta(\log)$.
problems.full_statement: In the bin packing problem, items of given sizes must be packed into unit-capacity bins, minimizing the number of bins used. The standard strong LP relaxation is the Gilmore-Gomory configuration LP. For a bin packing instance $I$, let $\mathrm{OPT}_f(I)$ be the value of the Gilmore-Gomory LP relaxation and $\mathrm{OPT}(I)$ the integer optimum. Define the additive integrality gap $$\mathrm{gap}=\sup_I\big(\mathrm{OPT}(I)-\mathrm{OPT}_f(I)\big).$$ The question is whether $\mathrm{gap}=O(1)$ (a constant number of extra bins regardless of instance). The related Modified Integer Round-Up Property (MIRUP) conjecture posits $\mathrm{OPT}(I)\le\lceil \mathrm{OPT}_f(I)\rceil+1$. The best known upper bound is $\mathrm{gap}=O(\log \mathrm{OPT}_f)$. Determine whether the additive integrality gap is $O(1)$ or $\Theta(\log)$.
problems.known_results: Hoberg and Rothvoss proved that the integer optimum is at most $\mathrm{OPT}_f+O(\log \mathrm{OPT}_f)$, improving the earlier $O(\log^2 \mathrm{OPT}_f)$ additive bound of Karmarkar and Karp. On the lower-bound side, no family is known with additive gap growing beyond a constant, and the stronger MIRUP conjecture predicts an extremely small constant gap. The main open question is whether the true additive gap is bounded by $O(1)$ or whether the logarithmic upper bound is qualitatively necessary.

problems.title: Optimal competitive ratio for online makespan scheduling
problems.short_statement: Jobs arrive one at a time and each must be placed irrevocably on one of $m$ identical machines, minimizing the makespan. What is the optimal competitive ratio of a deterministic online algorithm as $m$ grows large?
problems.open_problem: Determine the exact asymptotic deterministic competitive ratio for online makespan scheduling on identical machines.
problems.full_statement: In online makespan scheduling on identical machines ($P\,|\,online\text{-}list\,|\,C_{\max}$), jobs arrive one at a time and each must be assigned irrevocably to one of $m$ identical machines upon arrival. The objective is to minimize the makespan, i.e., the load (total processing time) of the busiest machine, compared against the optimal offline assignment. Graham's 1966 greedy (list scheduling) rule is $(2-1/m)$-competitive. Let $\rho^*(m)$ be the optimal competitive ratio of a deterministic online algorithm on $m$ machines, and let $\rho^*=\lim_{m\to\infty}\rho^*(m)$ be its asymptotic value. Determine $\rho^*$. Known: $1.88\le \rho^* \le 1.9201$.
problems.known_results: Graham's list-scheduling algorithm is $(2-1/m)$-competitive, and this is optimal only for $m=2$ and $m=3$. For large $m$, Fleischer and Wahl gave the best known deterministic upper bound, about $1.9201$, while Rudin's lower-bound construction gives about $1.88$. The asymptotic interval $[1.88,1.9201]$ has been stable for many years. The open problem is to determine the exact asymptotic deterministic competitive ratio for online makespan scheduling on identical machines.

problems.title: Optimal competitive ratio for randomized list update
problems.short_statement: Maintaining an unsorted linked list under access requests (access cost = position, accessed item may move toward the front for free), what is the optimal competitive ratio of a randomized algorithm against an oblivious adversary?
problems.open_problem: Determine the optimal randomized competitive ratio for the list update problem against an oblivious adversary.
problems.full_statement: In the list update problem, an algorithm maintains an unsorted linked list of items under a sequence of access requests. Accessing the item currently in position $i$ costs $i$; immediately after an access, the accessed item may be moved toward the front of the list for free (free transpositions of the accessed item), while other rearrangements cost per transposition (partial cost model). For deterministic algorithms the optimal competitive ratio is exactly $2$ (settled). Let $r^*$ be the optimal competitive ratio of a randomized online list-update algorithm against an oblivious adversary in the partial cost model. Determine $r^*$. Known: $1.50084 \le r^* \le 1.6$. Moreover, every 'projective' algorithm has ratio $\ge 1.6$.
problems.known_results: The deterministic version is completely understood: Sleator and Tarjan proved the tight competitive ratio is exactly $2$. For randomized algorithms against an oblivious adversary, the best upper bound remains the COMB algorithm of Albers, von Stengel, and Werchner with ratio $1.6$, while the best lower bound is $1.50084$. Known high-performing algorithms are projective, and projective algorithms cannot beat $1.6$. The open problem is to determine whether a non-projective randomized algorithm can improve on $1.6$, or whether the optimal randomized ratio is closer to the current lower bound.

problems.title: Deterministic competitive ratio for the hard k-taxi problem
problems.short_statement: In the hard $k$-taxi problem, $k$ taxis serve pickup-dropoff requests and only empty (passenger-free) driving is charged. Does any finite deterministic competitive ratio exist for general $k$ on every metric space?
problems.open_problem: Determine whether any finite deterministic competitive ratio exists for general $k$ on arbitrary metrics.
problems.full_statement: The $k$-taxi problem generalizes $k$-server: $k$ taxis are located in a metric space, and each request is a pair $(s,t)$ of points; to serve it, some taxi must travel to the pickup point $s$ and then carry the passenger to the dropoff point $t$. In the hard version of the problem, the cost counts only the distance a taxi travels without a passenger (the 'empty' driving); the distance traveled while carrying a passenger (from $s$ to $t$) is free. This generalizes $k$-server, which is recovered by taking $s=t$. The question is whether there exists any finite deterministic competitive ratio (for instance, of the form $2^{O(k)}$) for every fixed $k$ on every metric space. The known lower bound is $\Omega(2^k)$, and finiteness of the deterministic competitive ratio is established only for $k\le 3$. Determine whether any finite deterministic competitive ratio exists for general $k$ on arbitrary metrics.
problems.known_results: Deterministic hard k-taxi: lower bound $\Omega(2^k)$; a finite competitive ratio is known only for small k. Whether any finite deterministic ratio exists for general k on general metrics is open. The randomized version was largely settled to poly-log.

problems.title: Optimal competitive ratio for general convex body chasing
problems.short_statement: Convex bodies in $\mathbb{R}^d$ arrive online and after each you must move your point into the current body, paying distance moved. For general (arbitrary) convex bodies, is the optimal competitive ratio $\Theta(\sqrt{d})$?
problems.open_problem: Determine the optimal competitive ratio for general convex body chasing; in particular, is it $\Theta(\sqrt d)$?
problems.full_statement: In convex body chasing, convex bodies $K_1,K_2,\dots\subseteq\mathbb{R}^d$ are revealed online one at a time. After each body $K_n$ is revealed, the algorithm must choose a point $x_n\in K_n$, paying movement cost $\|x_n-x_{n-1}\|$. The competitive ratio is $$\sup \frac{\sum_n\|x_n-x_{n-1}\|}{\mathrm{OPT}},$$ where $\mathrm{OPT}$ is the optimal offline cost. The nested case (each body contained in the previous, $K_1\supseteq K_2\supseteq\cdots$) is essentially settled at $\Theta(\sqrt{d})$. For general (arbitrary) convex bodies in Euclidean space, the best upper bound is $O(\min(d,\sqrt{d\log N}))$ (where $N$ is the number of bodies) and the lower bound is $\Omega(\sqrt d)$. Determine the optimal competitive ratio for general convex body chasing; in particular, is it $\Theta(\sqrt d)$?
problems.known_results: The Friedman-Linial conjecture (a finite ratio depending only on d exists) is resolved: Sellke and Argue-Gupta-Guruganesh-Tang achieve $O(d)$. Nested chasing is essentially tight at $\Theta(\sqrt{d})$. For general convex body chasing in Euclidean space, the best upper bound is O(min(d, sqrt(d log N))) versus $\Omega(\sqrt{d})$; the exact ratio remains open.

problems.title: Cardinality dependence in submodular maximization with bandit feedback
problems.short_statement: Repeatedly picking a subset of at most $k$ of $n$ items and observing only a noisy value of an unknown monotone submodular function, competing against the $(1-1/e)$ greedy benchmark, what is the exact dependence of the minimax regret on the cardinality budget $k$?
problems.open_problem: Determine the exact cardinality ($k$) dependence of the minimax regret for general algorithms; i.e., close the $L^{1/3}$ (lower bound) versus $L$ (upper bound) gap.
problems.full_statement: Consider repeated subset selection with full-bandit feedback against a monotone submodular function. There is a monotone submodular function $f:2^{[n]}\to[0,1]$ over $n$ items, unknown to the learner. In each round the learner selects a subset of at most $k$ items (a cardinality-$k$ constraint) and observes only a noisy (sub-Gaussian) value of $f$ on the chosen set (full-bandit feedback). Performance is measured by minimax regret against the $(1-1/e)$ greedy benchmark over a horizon $T$. The dependence of the regret on the horizon $T$ ($T^{2/3}$) and on the number of items $n$ ($n^{1/3}$) is essentially settled. The dependence on the cardinality budget $k$ is not: the proven lower bound carries an $L^{1/3}$ factor while the best algorithm (Sub-UCB) carries an $L$ factor, minimizing over a split parameter $L\le k$. Determine the exact cardinality ($k$) dependence of the minimax regret for general algorithms; i.e., close the $L^{1/3}$ (lower bound) versus $L$ (upper bound) gap.
problems.known_results: The minimax dependence on the time horizon and ground-set size is essentially understood: the regret scales as $T^{2/3}$ in time and has the known $n^{1/3}$ dependence. The unresolved part is the dependence on the cardinality constraint $L$. Current lower bounds give an $L^{1/3}$ dependence, while the best general upper bounds are linear in $L$; Tajdini, Jain, and Jamieson match the linear dependence only for a restricted algorithm class. The open problem is to close this cardinality gap for unrestricted algorithms.

problems.title: Existence of EFX allocations (four or more agents)
problems.short_statement: An EFX allocation of indivisible goods is one where any envy agent $i$ feels toward agent $j$ vanishes after removing any single good from $j$'s bundle. Does a complete EFX allocation always exist for four or more agents, even with additive valuations?
problems.open_problem: does a complete EFX allocation exist for every instance with $n\ge 4$ additive agents?
problems.full_statement: Let $N=\{1,\dots,n\}$ be a set of agents and $M$ a set of indivisible goods, with each agent $i$ having an additive valuation $v_i$. An allocation $(X_1,\dots,X_n)$ partitioning $M$ among the agents is EFX (envy-free up to any good) if for all agents $i,j$ and every good $g\in X_j$ with $v_i(\{g\})>0$: $v_i(X_i)\ge v_i(X_j\setminus\{g\})$. In words, any envy that agent $i$ feels toward agent $j$ would vanish upon removing any single (positively valued) good from $j$'s bundle. Exact complete EFX allocations are known to always exist for up to 3 agents with additive valuations. Question: does a complete EFX allocation exist for every instance with $n\ge 4$ additive agents?
problems.known_results: For additive goods, exact EFX allocations are known to exist for at most three agents, by Chaudhury, Garg, and Mehlhorn. For four agents, Berger and coauthors prove an almost-complete EFX allocation in which at most one good is left unallocated. For general $n$, EFX with charity is known, meaning that fewer than $n$ goods may be donated, and a $0.618$-EFX allocation always exists. The open problem is whether a complete exact EFX allocation always exists for additive goods when $n\ge 4$.

problems.title: Existence of EFX allocations for chores (three agents)
problems.short_statement: For indivisible chores (which impose costs), an EFX allocation is one where any envy agent $i$ feels toward $j$ vanishes after dropping any single chore from $i$'s own bundle. Does an EFX allocation always exist for additive chores with three agents?
problems.open_problem: does an EFX allocation always exist for chores with additive costs and $n=3$ agents?
problems.full_statement: Let $N=\{1,\dots,n\}$ be a set of agents and $M$ a set of indivisible chores, with each agent $i$ having an additive cost function $c_i$ (chores impose costs / disutilities). An allocation $(X_1,\dots,X_n)$ partitioning $M$ is EFX (for chores) if for all agents $i,j$ and every chore $e\in X_i$ with $c_i(\{e\})>0$: $c_i(X_i\setminus\{e\})\le c_i(X_j)$. In words, any envy agent $i$ feels toward agent $j$ vanishes upon dropping any single (positively costly) chore from $i$'s own bundle. Unlike the goods setting, the situation for chores is harsher: with additive costs and four or more agents, EFX allocations can provably fail to exist. Question: does an EFX allocation always exist for chores with additive costs and $n=3$ agents?
problems.known_results: For superadditive cost functions, EFX allocations can fail to exist even with three agents. For additive costs, nonexistence examples are known for some number of agents $n\ge 4$, so the general chores analogue of the goods conjecture is false. The remaining small case of additive chores with exactly three agents is still open. Positive results are known when the number of chores is at most $n+2$, for identical-ordering costs, and for bi-valued costs; in general, a $2$-EFX allocation always exists for additive chores.

problems.title: Optimal approximation factor for Nash Social Welfare
problems.short_statement: Nash Social Welfare is the geometric mean of agents' utilities; maximizing it over indivisible goods is NP-hard. What is the best constant-factor polynomial-time approximation, for additive, submodular, and subadditive valuations?
problems.open_problem: Determine $\inf\{\alpha:$ a poly-time $\alpha$-approximation exists$\}$ for each valuation class in the hierarchy additive $\subset$ submodular $\subset$ XOS $\subset$ subadditive.
problems.full_statement: There is a set of agents $N$ and a set of indivisible goods $M$, with each agent $i$ having a valuation $v_i$. For an allocation $A=(A_1,\dots,A_n)$ the Nash Social Welfare is the geometric mean of the agents' utilities, $$\mathrm{NSW}(A)=\left(\prod_{i\in N} v_i(A_i)\right)^{1/n}.$$ A polynomial-time algorithm is an $\alpha$-approximation (for $\alpha\ge 1$) if $\mathrm{NSW}(A)\ge \mathrm{OPT}/\alpha$ on all instances, where $\mathrm{OPT}$ is the maximum Nash Social Welfare. Maximizing NSW exactly is NP-hard. Determine $\inf\{\alpha:$ a poly-time $\alpha$-approximation exists$\}$ for each valuation class in the hierarchy additive $\subset$ submodular $\subset$ XOS $\subset$ subadditive. For additive valuations the best algorithm achieves $\approx 1.45$ while hardness is only $\approx 1.07$.
problems.known_results: Additive: best algorithm $e^{1/e}$~1.445; APX-hardness ~1.069; the configuration LP has integrality gap exactly $e^{1/e}$, so the true constant lies in [1.069, 1.445] and is open. Submodular: a constant factor is known (~3.56); hardness $e/(e-1)$~1.582. Subadditive: a constant factor exists (large/unoptimized). No conjectured exact constant in any class.

problems.title: Best approximation factor for Maximin Share (MMS) fair division
problems.short_statement: An agent's maximin share is the value of the worst bundle when she partitions all goods into $n$ bundles. Since exact MMS allocations need not exist, what is the largest constant $\alpha$ for which $\alpha$-MMS allocations are always guaranteed?
problems.open_problem: Determine $\alpha^*=\sup\{\alpha:$ an $\alpha$-MMS allocation exists for every additive instance$\}$.
problems.full_statement: There is a set of agents $N=\{1,\dots,n\}$ and a set of goods $M$, with each agent $i$ having an additive valuation $v_i$. Let $\Pi_n(M)$ be the set of all partitions of $M$ into $n$ bundles. Agent $i$'s maximin share is $$\mathrm{MMS}_i=\max_{(B_1,\dots,B_n)\in\Pi_n(M)}\min_j v_i(B_j),$$ i.e., the best value she can guarantee herself if she partitions all goods into $n$ bundles and is then forced to take the worst (lowest-value) bundle. An allocation $A=(A_1,\dots,A_n)$ is $\alpha$-MMS if $v_i(A_i)\ge\alpha\cdot\mathrm{MMS}_i$ for all agents $i$. Exact ($\alpha=1$) MMS allocations provably do not always exist, even with additive valuations. Determine $\alpha^*=\sup\{\alpha:$ an $\alpha$-MMS allocation exists for every additive instance$\}$.
problems.known_results: The best universal guarantee has improved from $2/3$ to $3/4$, then to $3/4+1/(12n)$, and more recently to $3/4+3/3836$ by Akrami and Garg, with further improvements moving the constant toward about $0.77$. On the impossibility side, Feige, Sapir, and Tauber show that no guarantee better than $1-1/n^4$ is always achievable for $n\ge 4$, and give a $39/40$ upper bound for three agents. The open problem is to determine the exact largest constant $\alpha$ for which every additive instance admits an $\alpha$-MMS allocation.

problems.title: Competition complexity of multi-item auctions (general regime)
problems.short_statement: How many extra i.i.d. bidders does a simple mechanism (sell items separately, or VCG) need to match the optimal revenue of the original bidders, in the multi-item setting? The additive case with fewer items than bidders is settled; other regimes are open.
problems.open_problem: exact bounds (including constants) in the regime $m\ge n$, for non-additive valuations (XOS / subadditive / unit-demand), and for correlated items. Determine the competition complexity in these regimes.
problems.full_statement: Fix a distribution $D$ over $m$ items. For $n$ bidders drawn i.i.d. from $D$, the competition complexity $C(n,m)$ is the smallest integer $c$ such that the revenue of a simple mechanism with $n+c$ bidders matches the optimal revenue with the original $n$ bidders; concretely, $\mathrm{SRev}_{n+c}(D^m)\ge \mathrm{Rev}_n(D^m)$, where $\mathrm{SRev}$ is the revenue from selling items separately (and VCG is the analogue) and $\mathrm{Rev}$ is the optimal revenue. This is the multi-item generalization of the Bulow-Klemperer result, which for a single item shows that recruiting one extra bidder lets a simple second-price auction match the optimal auction on the original bidders. The additive/independent case is tight: $C(n,m)=\Theta(\sqrt{nm})$ for $m<n$ and $\Theta(n\ln(1+m/n))$ for $n\le m$. Open: exact bounds (including constants) in the regime $m\ge n$, for non-additive valuations (XOS / subadditive / unit-demand), and for correlated items. Determine the competition complexity in these regimes.
problems.known_results: For additive buyers with independent item values, the order of competition complexity is known: it is $\Theta(\sqrt{nm})$ when $m<n$ and $\Theta(n\ln(1+m/n))$ when $n\le m$. These results are tight up to constant factors. The open problems are to determine the exact constants, sharpen the $m\ge n$ regime, and extend the theory beyond independent additive values to non-additive valuation classes and correlated item values.

problems.title: Menu complexity of approximately revenue-optimal multi-item auctions
problems.short_statement: When selling several items to one additive buyer, how many distinct menu (lottery) options are needed to capture a $(1-\epsilon)$ fraction of the optimal revenue, as a function of the number of items $n$ (and the exact two-item exponent)?
problems.open_problem: the tight growth of $C(n,\varepsilon)$ in $n$ for fixed $\varepsilon$ (in particular whether $\mathrm{poly}(n)$ suffices), and the exact two-item menu-size exponent. Determine these.
problems.full_statement: Consider a single additive buyer whose item values are drawn from independent distributions $F_1,\dots,F_n$ over $n$ items. A mechanism can be described as a menu of (possibly randomized) options. Let $C(n,\varepsilon)$ be the minimum menu size such that some mechanism with at most $C(n,\varepsilon)$ entries achieves expected revenue $\ge(1-\varepsilon)\mathrm{Rev}(F)$ for every such product distribution $F$, where $\mathrm{Rev}(F)$ is the optimal revenue. Even with two items the revenue-optimal menu can require a continuum of options. Known: $C(n,1/n)\ge 2^{\Omega(n)}$; for two items the menu size is between $\Omega(\varepsilon^{-1/4})$ and $O(\varepsilon^{-2})$. Open: the tight growth of $C(n,\varepsilon)$ in $n$ for fixed $\varepsilon$ (in particular whether $\mathrm{poly}(n)$ suffices), and the exact two-item menu-size exponent. Determine these.
problems.known_results: Several qualitative questions are resolved: finite menus can approximate optimal revenue to within a $(1-\epsilon)$ factor, and the better of selling items separately and selling the grand bundle gives a constant-factor approximation. For two items, the communication complexity is $\Theta(\log(1/\epsilon))$. The open problem is quantitative menu-size growth: for fixed $\epsilon$, it is unknown whether a polynomial-size menu in $n$ always suffices, since exponential lower bounds are known only when $\epsilon=1/n$. Even for two items, the exact exponent in the dependence on $1/\epsilon$ remains open.

problems.title: Price of anonymity in multi-unit auctions
problems.short_statement: An anonymous mechanism must offer every buyer the same price; how much revenue does that cost versus the optimal (Myerson) mechanism? For a single item the tight gap is $\approx 2.62$; for selling multiple identical units the exact gap is not fully pinned down.
problems.open_problem: the exact constants $\rho_k$ for finite $k>1$ beyond the regular / large-market regime, and the multi-item analogues. Determine these.
problems.full_statement: Consider revenue maximization with $n$ buyers whose value distributions differ. The optimal (Myerson) mechanism may charge different buyers different prices, whereas an anonymous mechanism must offer everyone the same price. For $k$ identical units and $n$ independent, non-identical regular value distributions, the price of anonymity is $$\rho_k=\sup_F \frac{\mathrm{OPT}(F)}{\mathrm{AP}(F)},$$ where $\mathrm{OPT}(F)$ is the optimal (Myerson) revenue and $\mathrm{AP}(F)$ is the best anonymous posted-price revenue, and the supremum is over all valid instances $F$. For a single item ($k=1$) the tight value is $\rho_1\approx 2.62$. Open: the exact constants $\rho_k$ for finite $k>1$ beyond the regular / large-market regime, and the multi-item analogues. Determine these.
problems.known_results: For one item, the price of anonymity is known exactly up to numerical evaluation: $\rho_1\approx 2.6202$. In multi-unit settings with general regular distributions, the cross-group gap has order $\Theta(\log k)$, while in large regular markets the ratio is $2+O(1/\sqrt{k})$ and converges to $2$. The open problem is to determine the exact constants for finite $k$ outside the large-market regime and to understand the corresponding anonymity loss for multi-item mechanisms.

problems.title: Maximum number of stable matchings in an n x n instance
problems.short_statement: Let $f(n)$ be the largest possible number of stable matchings over all $n\times n$ stable-marriage instances. It grows exponentially, but the exact base is unknown, with bounds between about $2.28^n$ and $3.55^n$.
problems.open_problem: Determine $\lim_{n\to\infty} f(n)^{1/n}$, the growth base of this exponential.
problems.full_statement: Consider the stable-marriage problem with $n$ men and $n$ women, each having a strict preference ordering over the members of the opposite side; a matching is stable if no man-woman pair both prefer each other to their assigned partners. A single instance can admit many stable matchings simultaneously. Let $f(n)$ be the maximum, over all $n\times n$ stable-marriage instances $I$, of the number of stable matchings $|S(I)|$. It is known that $f(n)=2^{\Theta(n)}$. Determine $\lim_{n\to\infty} f(n)^{1/n}$, the growth base of this exponential. Currently it is known that $\Omega(2.28^n)\le f(n)\le O(3.55^n)$. This problem was first posed by Knuth in the 1970s.
problems.known_results: Lower bound $\Omega(2.28^n)$ (Irving-Leather; extended by Thurber). Upper bound $O(3.55^n)$ (Palmer-Palvolgyi 2021), improving the first simply-exponential bound of Karlin-Oveis Gharan-Weber 2018. Exact base open; gap [2.28, 3.55].

problems.title: Existence threshold for stable matching with couples
problems.short_statement: In hospitals/residents with couples (who submit joint preferences over pairs of positions), a stable matching can fail to exist and deciding existence is NP-complete. What is the precise rate at which the number of couples can grow with market size while a stable matching still exists w.h.p.?
problems.open_problem: Determine the tight scaling threshold (and constants) for the number of couples, as a function of market size $n$, separating the regime where a stable matching exists w.h.p.
problems.full_statement: In the Hospitals/Residents problem with Couples, couples rank ordered pairs of hospitals (joint preferences over pairs of positions), introducing complementarities absent from the classical problem. Unlike the classic stable-matching problem, a stable matching need not exist (Roth 1984), and deciding existence is NP-complete (Ronn 1990). Yet in large real markets such as the medical residency match, stable outcomes are almost always found. Consider random large markets of size $n$: it is known that if the number of couples is $o(\sqrt{n})$ a stable matching exists with high probability, whereas if the number of couples grows linearly in $n$ (i.e., $\Theta(n)$), with constant probability no stable matching exists. Determine the tight scaling threshold (and constants) for the number of couples, as a function of market size $n$, separating the regime where a stable matching exists w.h.p. from the regime where none exists with constant probability; and identify the sharp preference-domain conditions.
problems.known_results: The qualitative landscape is well established: stable matchings can fail to exist with couples, and deciding existence is NP-complete. In large random markets, stable matchings exist with high probability under sufficiently sparse couples, including the $o(\sqrt n)$ regime, while with a linear number of couples nonexistence occurs with constant probability. The open problem is to identify the sharp threshold between these regimes, including constants and the precise preference-domain assumptions under which stability is likely.

problems.title: Tight competitive ratio of the prophet secretary problem
problems.short_statement: A gambler faces $n$ independent rewards from known but different distributions arriving in uniformly random order, accepting or irrevocably rejecting each and keeping at most one. What is the exact best-possible competitive ratio against the expected maximum?
problems.open_problem: Determine the exact prophet-secretary constant for independent non-identical rewards arriving in uniformly random order.
problems.full_statement: Let $X_1,\dots,X_n$ be independent random rewards with known but possibly different distributions $X_i\sim F_i$. They are revealed to the gambler one at a time in uniformly random order; upon seeing each value the gambler must irrevocably accept it (stopping) or reject it, keeping at most one value. The benchmark is the prophet's expected maximum $\mathbb{E}[\max_i X_i]$. Define the optimal prophet-secretary competitive ratio $$\alpha^*=\inf_{\{F_i\}}\sup_{\text{online rules}}\frac{\mathbb{E}[X_{\text{selected}}]}{\mathbb{E}[\max_i X_i]}.$$ With adversarial order the best guarantee is exactly $1/2$; uniformly random order provably helps. Determine $\alpha^*$. Known: $0.688\le\alpha^*\le 0.7235$. (This is distinct from the i.i.d. case, which is tight at $\approx 0.745$, and from the order-selection model, $\approx 0.7258$.)
problems.known_results: The best current algorithmic lower bound is $0.688$, improving on the original $1-1/e$ guarantee of Esfandiari and coauthors. The best analytically proven upper bound in the random-order model is $0.7235$, with a tighter computer-assisted bound around $0.7254$ also known in related formulations. None of these bounds is believed to be tight. The open problem is to determine the exact prophet-secretary constant.

problems.title: Tight competitive ratio of online bipartite matching under random arrival
problems.short_statement: With offline vertices known in advance and online vertices arriving in uniformly random order (matched immediately and irrevocably), what is the exact best-possible competitive ratio, and what is the exact ratio of the canonical RANKING algorithm?
problems.open_problem: Determine the optimal competitive ratio for online bipartite matching under uniformly random arrivals, and the exact ratio of RANKING in this model.
problems.full_statement: Let $G=(L,R,E)$ be a bipartite graph in which the vertices $L$ are offline (known in advance) and the vertices of $R$ arrive online one at a time in uniformly random order. Upon arrival, each online vertex reveals its edges to $L$ and must be matched immediately and irrevocably to an available offline neighbor (or left unmatched). The objective is to maximize the cardinality of the matching, compared against the maximum matching $\mathrm{OPT}(G)$. In the worst-case (adversarial) arrival model the optimal competitive ratio is exactly $1-1/e$; with uniformly random arrival order, randomness provably helps. Define $$\rho^*=\inf_G\sup_{\text{online algs}}\frac{\mathbb{E}[|\text{ALG}|]}{|\text{OPT}(G)|}.$$ Determine $\rho^*$ (and the sub-question: the exact competitive ratio of the canonical RANKING algorithm in this random-order model). Known: $0.696\le\rho^*\le 0.8206$.
problems.known_results: Lower bound: RANKING achieves >= 0.696 in random order (Mahdian-Yan 2011). Upper bound: ~0.8206 (Chierichetti et al., ICALP 2025, improving 0.823 of Manshadi-Oveis Gharan-Saberi). Exact optimal ratio open with a large gap.

where_posed.text: Model and adversarial-optimal result from Karp, Vazirani, Vazirani, 'An Optimal Algorithm for On-line Bipartite Matching', 1990, STOC.

problems.title: Computing EF1 and Pareto-optimal allocations in polynomial time
problems.short_statement: For indivisible goods with additive valuations, an allocation that is both EF1 (envy-free up to one good) and Pareto-optimal always exists, but the known algorithm is only pseudo-polynomial. Can such an allocation be computed in truly polynomial time?
problems.open_problem: Determine whether a truly polynomial-time algorithm exists.
problems.full_statement: There are $n$ agents with additive valuations $v_i:2^M\to\mathbb{Z}_{\ge0}$ over a set $M$ of $m$ indivisible goods, with the valuations given in binary. An allocation is EF1 (envy-free up to one good) if any agent's envy toward another vanishes after removing some single good from the envied bundle, and (fractionally) Pareto-optimal if no other (fractional) allocation makes some agent better off without making another worse off. Such an allocation always exists (the maximum-Nash-welfare allocation is one), so the question is purely computational: is there an algorithm running in time $\mathrm{poly}(n,m,\log V_{\max})$ (where $V_{\max}$ is the largest value) that outputs an allocation that is simultaneously EF1 and fractionally Pareto-optimal? The known algorithm runs in only pseudo-polynomial time (polynomial in the magnitude of values, not their bit-length). The problem is in PLS and is not known to be PLS-hard, NP-hard, or in P. Determine whether a truly polynomial-time algorithm exists.
problems.known_results: Upper bound: pseudo-polynomial (Barman-Krishnamurthy-Vaish 2018); truly polynomial for a constant number of agents or a constant number of distinct values. No lower bound known (not known NP-hard or PLS-hard). Listed as an open problem in the Aziz-Li-Moulin-Wu survey.

where_posed.text: Pseudo-polynomial algorithm and the open computational question from Barman, Krishnamurthy, Vaish, 'Finding Fair and Efficient Allocations', 2018, EC; listed open in the Aziz-Li-Moulin-Wu survey.

problems.title: Query complexity of envy-free cake cutting
problems.short_statement: In the Robertson-Webb query model for envy-free cake cutting, a bounded protocol exists but uses a tower of six exponentials in $n$ queries, while the best lower bound is only quadratic. What is the true query complexity?
problems.open_problem: Determine the true asymptotic growth of $\mathrm{RW}(n)$; even an elementary (tower-free) upper bound or a super-quadratic lower bound is open.
problems.full_statement: In cake cutting, a divisible good (the cake, modeled as $[0,1]$) is to be divided among $n$ agents with general nonatomic additive valuations. In the Robertson-Webb query model, a protocol learns agents' valuations only through 'cut' queries (asking an agent to mark a point giving a specified value) and 'evaluate' queries (asking an agent the value of a specified interval). An allocation is envy-free if no agent prefers another agent's piece to her own. Let $\mathrm{RW}(n)$ be the worst-case number of Robertson-Webb queries required by any deterministic protocol that returns an envy-free allocation of $[0,1]$ among $n$ agents. A finite, bounded envy-free protocol exists for every $n$ (Aziz-Mackenzie), but its query bound is a tower of six exponentials in $n$; the best lower bound is only quadratic. Known: $\mathrm{RW}(n)=\Omega(n^2)$ and $\mathrm{RW}(n)\le n^{n^{n^{n^{n^{n}}}}}$. Determine the true asymptotic growth of $\mathrm{RW}(n)$; even an elementary (tower-free) upper bound or a super-quadratic lower bound is open.
problems.known_results: Procaccia proved the best general lower bound, $\Omega(n^2)$ Robertson-Webb queries. Aziz and Mackenzie gave the first finite bounded protocol for every number of agents, but the upper bound is a tower of six exponentials in $n$. This is one of the largest known gaps in fair division; substantial progress is known only for special cases such as four agents. The open problem is to determine the true asymptotic query complexity, or even to obtain either a tower-free upper bound or a super-quadratic lower bound.

where_posed.text: Bounded protocol with tower-of-exponentials query bound in Aziz, Mackenzie, 'A Discrete and Bounded Envy-Free Cake Cutting Protocol for Any Number of Agents', 2016, FOCS (CACM 2020).

problems.title: Best approximation ratio for maximum stable matching with ties
problems.short_statement: In stable marriage with ties and incomplete lists (SMTI), finding a maximum-cardinality weakly stable matching is NP-hard. What is the optimal polynomial-time approximation ratio?
problems.open_problem: Determine the optimal polynomial-time approximation ratio $\alpha^*$ for MAX-SMTI.
problems.full_statement: In the stable marriage problem with ties and incomplete lists (SMTI), agents rank only some potential partners (incomplete lists) and may be indifferent among several (ties). A matching is weakly stable if there is no pair who both strictly prefer each other to their assigned partners. Because of ties and incompleteness, weakly stable matchings can have different sizes, and finding a maximum-cardinality one (MAX-SMTI) is NP-hard (indeed APX-hard). Determine the optimal polynomial-time approximation ratio $\alpha^*$ for MAX-SMTI. Known: $\tfrac{4}{3}\le\alpha^*\le\tfrac{3}{2}$ assuming the Unique Games Conjecture (UGC), and $\tfrac{33}{29}\le\alpha^*\le\tfrac{3}{2}$ unconditionally.
problems.known_results: The best general approximation algorithm has ratio $3/2$, with analyses due to McDermid and related work of Paluch and Kiraly. On the hardness side, no $(4/3-\epsilon)$-approximation exists under the Unique Games Conjecture, and an unconditional NP-hardness bound of $33/29\approx 1.138$ is known. The open problem is to determine the exact approximation threshold for MAX-SMTI, which currently lies between $4/3$ and $3/2$ under UGC and between $33/29$ and $3/2$ unconditionally.

where_posed.text: 3/2-approximation in McDermid, 'A 3/2-Approximation Algorithm for General Stable Marriage', 2009, ICALP.

problems.title: Minimum subsidy for envy-freeness with monotone valuations
problems.short_statement: Indivisible goods plus cash subsidies can eliminate envy. For additive valuations with item values in $[0,1]$, one dollar per agent suffices and is tight. What is the minimum per-agent subsidy for general monotone valuations?
problems.open_problem: Determine $s^*(n)$ for general monotone valuations.
problems.full_statement: When indivisible goods cannot be divided fairly, monetary subsidies can eliminate envy: some agents are given cash so that nobody prefers another agent's bundle-plus-cash to their own. Consider $n$ agents with monotone valuations $v_i:2^M\to\mathbb{R}_{\ge0}$ satisfying the marginal bound $v_i(S\cup\{e\})-v_i(S)\le 1$ for all $S$ and $e$ (each item adds at most $1$). Let $s^*(n)$ be the smallest value such that every instance admits an allocation $(X_1,\dots,X_n)$ together with per-agent subsidies $p_i\ge0$ satisfying $\max_i p_i\le s^*(n)$ that is envy-free: $v_i(X_i)+p_i\ge v_i(X_j)+p_j$ for all agents $i,j$. For additive valuations the tight total subsidy is $n-1$ (i.e., one dollar per agent on average suffices and is tight). Determine $s^*(n)$ for general monotone valuations.
problems.known_results: Additive: total subsidy n-1 suffices and is tight (Brustle et al. 2020). Monotone (general): per-agent upper bound improved to < n-1.5 (total ~ ($n^2$-n-1)/2); lower bound only n-1 total. The factor gap for monotone valuations is open; conjectured target is the additive bound.

where_posed.text: Additive tight bound and the monotone question from Brustle, Dippel, Narayan, Suzuki, Vetta, 'One Dollar Each Eliminates Envy', 2020, EC.

problems.title: Optimal policy structure for lost-sales inventory with positive lead time
problems.short_statement: For a periodic-review single-item inventory system with stochastic demand, lost sales, and a deterministic lead time $L \ge 1$, characterize the structure of the cost-minimizing policy. It is provably neither base-stock nor constant-order for $L \ge 1$, and no tractable characterization is known.
problems.open_problem: Characterize the exact optimal policy for periodic-review lost-sales inventory with positive deterministic lead time, or prove sharp non-asymptotic guarantees for a simple policy class.
problems.full_statement: A single item is reviewed each period $t$ over an infinite horizon under the long-run average-cost criterion. Demands $D_t$ are i.i.d. nonnegative with known distribution. An order $q_t \ge 0$ placed in period $t$ arrives after a deterministic lead time $L \in \mathbb{Z}_{\ge 1}$, so the relevant state is the pipeline vector $\mathbf{x}_t = (I_t, q_{t-L+1}, \dots, q_{t-1}) \in \mathbb{R}_{\ge 0}^{L}$, where $I_t$ is on-hand inventory; because the state is the entire vector of outstanding orders, the dynamic program is high-dimensional. With on-hand inventory after arrival $y_t$, demand is met up to availability and any remainder is LOST (not backlogged): $I_{t+1} = (y_t - D_t)^+$, with per-period cost $h\,(y_t - D_t)^+ + p\,(D_t - y_t)^+$ for holding cost $h>0$ and lost-sales penalty $p>0$. The objective is $\min_{\pi} \limsup_{T\to\infty} \frac{1}{T}\,\mathbb{E}^{\pi}\!\left[\sum_{t=1}^{T}\big(h\,I_{t+1} + p\,(D_t - y_t)^+\big)\right]$. $$\textbf{Open:}\quad \text{characterize the structure of an optimal policy } \pi^\star \text{ for } L \ge 1.$$ It is known that $\pi^\star$ is neither a base-stock nor a constant-order policy for $L\ge 1$ (Zipkin 2008), and the value function lacks the convexity that makes the backlog model tractable. What remains genuinely open: a usable characterization of $\pi^\star$, explicit non-asymptotic optimality gaps for simple policies at moderate $L$, and whether a single simple policy is simultaneously near-optimal across all parameter regimes. Only asymptotic results exist: base-stock policies as the penalty grows, and constant-order policies as $L$ grows.
problems.known_results: The optimal policy can in principle be computed by dynamic programming but is intractable for $L \ge 1$ due to the L-dimensional pipeline state, and its structure is uncharacterized; Zipkin (2008) shows it is neither base-stock nor constant-order. Two complementary asymptotic regimes are proven: (i) order-up-to/base-stock policies are asymptotically optimal as the lost-sales penalty p -> infinity (Huh, Janakiraman, Muckstadt, Rusmevichientong 2009); (ii) the constant-order policy is asymptotically optimal as the lead time L -> infinity (Goldberg et al. 2016), sharpened by Xin & Goldberg (2016) to an exponentially decaying optimality gap in L. Projected-inventory-level (PIL) policies are asymptotically optimal in BOTH regimes (van Jaarsveld & Arts 2024). What remains open: an exact characterization of the optimal policy for finite $L \ge 1$, tight non-asymptotic optimality gaps at moderate L, and whether any simple policy is simultaneously optimal across all regimes. The Goldberg-Reiman-Wang (2021) survey frames this whole line as asymptotic analysis of an otherwise intractable problem.

where_posed.text: Stated/originated in Zipkin, 'On the Structure of Lost-Sales Inventory Models,' Operations Research 56(4):937-944, 2008, which proves the optimal policy is neither base-stock nor constant-order; framed as an open line in the Goldberg-Reiman-Wang (2021) survey.

problems.title: Optimal policy structure for dual-sourcing inventory (fast/slow sources)
problems.short_statement: A firm replenishes a single product from a cheap slow source and an expensive fast source under stochastic demand and full backlogging. When the lead-time gap is at least 2, the optimal ordering policy's structure is unknown and believed intractable; whether any simple policy (Tailored Base-Surge, dual-index, single-index) is optimal is open.
problems.open_problem: Characterize the exact optimal policy for dual sourcing when the regular and expedited lead times differ by at least two periods.
problems.full_statement: A single item is reviewed each period over an infinite horizon (discounted or long-run average cost) with i.i.d. demands $D_t$. Two sources are available: a regular source with per-unit cost $c_r$ and deterministic lead time $L_r$, and an expedited source with cost $c_e > c_r$ and lead time $L_e$, where $L_r > L_e \ge 0$. In period $t$ the firm chooses order quantities $q^r_t, q^e_t \ge 0$; unmet demand is fully backlogged with holding cost $h$ and backorder penalty $b$. The state must encode both outstanding-order pipelines, of dimension $\sim (L_r - L_e)$, giving a high-dimensional MDP. A Tailored Base-Surge (TBS) policy fixes a constant regular order $q^r_t \equiv r$ and uses an order-up-to rule $q^e_t = (S - \widehat{IP}_t)^+$ at the expedited source, where $\widehat{IP}_t$ is the expedited inventory position. $$\textbf{Open:}\quad \text{characterize an optimal policy when the lead-time gap } L_r - L_e \ge 2.$$ For $L_r - L_e = 1$ the optimal expedited decision is a base-stock rule, but for $L_r - L_e \ge 2$ the optimal policy has no known simple structure and is conjectured intractable. What remains open: the exact structure of $\pi^\star$ for gap $\ge 2$; whether TBS, dual-index, or single-index policies are exactly optimal in any nontrivial case; and non-asymptotic guarantees for moderate $L_r - L_e$. Only an asymptotic result is known: TBS is asymptotically optimal as the regular lead time $L_r \to \infty$ with $L_e$ fixed.
problems.known_results: For lead-time gap $L_r$ - $L_e$ = 1, the optimal expedited policy is base-stock and the problem is tractable; for gap >= 2 the optimal structure is unknown and the problem has 'resisted solution for over 40 years.' Heuristics perform well numerically but are not optimal in general: the dual-index policy (Veeraraghavan & Scheller-Wolf 2008) and new policy classes (Sheopuri-Janakiraman-Seshadri 2010); TBS analyzed by Janakiraman-Seshadri-Sheopuri 2015. The central proven result is that TBS is asymptotically optimal as $L_r$ -> infinity with $L_e$ fixed (Xin & Goldberg 2018); a 2026 working paper (Optimization Online) further shows the TBS optimality gap decays exponentially in $L_r$. What remains open: the exact optimal policy for any finite gap >= 2; whether any single simple policy class is exactly optimal; and tight non-asymptotic guarantees for moderate $L_r$ - $L_e$. The Goldberg-Reiman-Wang (2021) survey presents dual-sourcing as a flagship case where only asymptotic characterizations are available.

problems.title: Optimal policy for one-warehouse multi-retailer (distribution) inventory systems
problems.short_statement: A single warehouse ships, with positive lead times, to several retailers each facing independent random demand. The optimal replenishment-and-allocation policy is unknown: echelon base-stock is optimal only under the artificial balance assumption (which permits negative allocations); without it, the true optimal structure is open.
problems.open_problem: Characterize the optimal replenishment-and-allocation policy for one-warehouse multi-retailer inventory under the true nonnegative-allocation constraints.
problems.full_statement: Consider a periodic-review, one-warehouse $N$-retailer distribution (divergent) system over an infinite horizon. Retailer $i$ faces i.i.d. demand $D_i$ per period, with deterministic lead times $L_0$ (supplier$\to$warehouse) and $L_i$ (warehouse$\to$retailer $i$). Unit holding costs $h_0 \ge 0$ at the warehouse and $h_i$ at retailer $i$, and unit backorder penalty $p_i$ at retailer $i$, are charged per period. Each period the warehouse orders $q_0 \ge 0$ and then allocates on-hand stock $y_0$ among retailers as shipments $q_i \ge 0$ subject to $\sum_{i=1}^N q_i \le y_0$ (no lateral transshipment, no return). Holding and backorder costs accrue at the retailers and holding cost at the warehouse. The objective is $\min \limsup_{T\to\infty}\frac1T\,\mathbb{E}\big[\sum_{t=1}^{T}(h_0 (y_0^t)^+ + \sum_{i=1}^N (h_i I_i^t + p_i B_i^t))\big]$ over admissible policies. Clark and Scarf (1960) proved echelon base-stock policies optimal for SERIAL systems, but here the difficulty is the allocation/imbalance problem: once stock is shipped down it cannot be moved sideways, so the warehouse cannot always achieve desired echelon positions. \textbf{Open:} Characterize the structure of an optimal policy, or prove none of a simple form exists. If the constraint $\sum_i q_i \le y_0$ is RELAXED to allow $q_i \in \mathbb{R}$ (the balance/allocation assumption), the problem decomposes and an echelon base-stock policy is optimal; under the true constraint $q_i \ge 0$, echelon base-stock is provably suboptimal and the optimal policy is unknown. Is there a polynomial-time-computable or closed-form characterization of the optimal (or a provably near-optimal, with explicit small constant) replenishment-and-allocation policy?
problems.known_results: Echelon base-stock policies are optimal ONLY under the balance/allocation assumption (Zipkin 1984; Federgruen-Zipkin 1984; Eppen-Schrage), which relaxes the no-lateral-transshipment constraint by permitting negative allocations. Under the true $q_i$ >= 0 constraint, echelon base-stock is provably suboptimal and the exact optimal policy is uncharacterized. For the deterministic-demand lot-sizing OWMR variant, Levi-Roundy-Shmoys-Sviridenko (2008) gave a 1.8-approximation via LP rounding; power-of-two policies give ~1.26; recent FPTAS-based guarantees exist (Operations Research 2024). For the stochastic divergent problem, only heuristics with numerically-assessed gaps and asymptotic-optimality results (e.g., Lagrangian policies for lost-sales multi-store systems) are known. A clean structural characterization, or a simple policy with an explicit small constant-factor guarantee for the stochastic backlog objective, remains open as of 2024-2025.

where_posed.text: Originates with Clark & Scarf, 'Optimal Policies for a Multi-Echelon Inventory Problem,' Management Science 6(4):475-490, 1960 (which solved the serial case); the divergent/distribution open problem and the imbalance obstruction are developed in Zipkin (1984) and Federgruen-Zipkin (1984), and surveyed in de Kok et al. (2018).

problems.title: Tractable optimal control of assembly systems from arbitrary (imbalanced) initial inventory
problems.short_statement: In an assembly system, components with their own suppliers and lead times combine to meet random demand. For arbitrary initial imbalance the optimum is a balanced echelon base-stock policy with time-varying levels (Chen-Muharremoglu); open is whether those dynamic levels admit a closed-form/efficient characterization, and the structure once fixed ordering costs are added.
problems.open_problem: Find a closed-form or polynomial-time characterization of the dynamic base-stock levels for general assembly systems, and understand what survives with positive fixed ordering costs.
problems.full_statement: Consider an $M$-component assembly system (serial-assembly / in-tree topology): component $j$ is procured with lead time $\ell_j$ and assembled into one finished unit. End demand is stochastic i.i.d.; linear holding costs apply at each stage and a backorder penalty at the finished-goods stage; the objective is long-run average (or discounted) cost. The issue is how to coordinate component orders when component inventories are out of balance. Rosling (1989) proves that under an initial-condition assumption ('long-run balance,' i.e. echelon inventory positions ordered consistently across components) the assembly system is equivalent to a serial system and a stationary balanced echelon base-stock policy with constant order-up-to levels $\{s_j\}$ is optimal. Chen-Muharremoglu (2014) extend optimality to ANY initial state: the optimal policy is a balanced echelon base-stock policy with dynamically evolving levels $\{s_j^t\}$, and Rosling's stationary policy is optimal whenever initial echelon inventory positions are non-decreasing in component index. \textbf{Open:} (i) Is there a closed-form or polynomial-time characterization of the time-varying levels $s_j^t$ under arbitrary initial imbalance (beyond an existence/structure result)? (ii) What is the optimal policy structure for assembly systems with positive lead times and fixed ordering costs $K_j>0$, where the serial reduction and base-stock optimality are not known to hold?
problems.known_results: Rosling (1989) reduced assembly systems (without setup costs) to serial systems under a 'long-run balance' initial condition, giving optimality of a stationary balanced echelon base-stock policy. Chen-Muharremoglu (2014, SSRN 2475218) closed the STRUCTURAL gap for arbitrary initial imbalance: the optimum is a balanced echelon base-stock policy with time-varying base-stock levels, and Rosling's stationary policy is optimal under the easy-to-check condition that initial echelon inventory positions are non-decreasing in item index. Thus the qualitative optimal-policy structure is essentially known. What remains genuinely open: (a) an explicit closed-form / efficient computation of the time-varying levels under arbitrary initial states; and (b) the optimal policy structure for assembly systems WITH fixed ordering costs (and general non-in-tree topologies with imbalance), where serial reduction is not known to apply. Confidence is medium because the headline structural problem is largely resolved; the open content is the narrower computational/closed-form and fixed-cost extensions.

where_posed.text: Originates with Rosling, 'Optimal Inventory Policies for Assembly Systems Under Random Demands,' Operations Research 37(4):565-579, 1989; the arbitrary-imbalance structural extension and the remaining open computational/fixed-cost gaps are framed in Chen & Muharremoglu, 'Optimal Policies for Assembly Systems: Completing Rosling's Characterization' (2014).

problems.title: Whittle index asymptotic optimality and the global attractor condition (Weber-Weiss)
problems.short_statement: Whittle (1988) conjectured his index policy is asymptotically optimal for restless bandits as the number of arms grows. Weber-Weiss (1990) proved this under indexability plus a global-attractor condition on the mean-field ODE, but gave a counterexample where it fails. Open: when the global-attractor/non-degeneracy conditions hold and the minimal assumptions for each optimality-gap rate.
problems.open_problem: Give checkable conditions under which Whittle's index policy is asymptotically optimal, and characterize when its optimality gap is $O(\sqrt N)$, $O(1)$, or exponentially small.
problems.full_statement:
Consider $N$ arms, each a two-action ($a\in\{0,1\}$) MDP with common kernels $P^0,P^1$ and rewards $R^0,R^1$ on a finite state space, under the hard constraint that exactly $\alpha N$ arms are activated each time, with the long-run average-reward criterion. Let $V^{\mathrm{opt}}(N)$ be the optimal value and $V^{\mathrm{rel}}(N)=Nho^{\mathrm{rel}}$ Whittle's relaxation value (the per-period constraint replaced by its time-average), which upper-bounds $V^{\mathrm{opt}}(N)$. A bandit is $\emph{indexable}$ if the set of states where the passive action is optimal in the $
u$-subsidized single-arm MDP is monotone non-decreasing in the subsidy $
u$; the Whittle index $
u_i$ is the smallest $
u$ making passive optimal in state $i$, and the Whittle Index Policy (WIP) activates the $\alpha N$ highest-index arms. Let $\mathbf m(t)\in\Delta^d$ be the empirical state distribution; under WIP it follows, as $N	o\infty$, a piecewise-affine mean-field drift $\dot{\mathbf m}=\phi(\mathbf m)$ with fixed point $\mathbf m^*$. $	extbf{Whittle's conjecture: }$ $V_{\mathrm{WIP}}(N)/N	oho^{\mathrm{rel}}$. $	extbf{Weber-Weiss (1990): }$ if the bandit is indexable and $\mathbf m^*$ is a $\emph{global attractor}$, then $V_{\mathrm{WIP}}(N)/N	oho^{\mathrm{rel}}$; and there exists an indexable instance whose ODE has a stable limit cycle (no global attractor) for which WIP is $\emph{not}$ asymptotically optimal. $	extbf{Open: }$ (a) a checkable characterization of when the global-attractor / Uniform Global Attractor Property holds; (b) the tight minimal-assumption characterization of when the gap is $O(\sqrt N)$ vs $O(1)$ vs $O(e^{-cN})$, and whether non-degeneracy/local stability are necessary for the exponential rate for index/priority-type policies.
problems.known_results: Proven: (i) Weber-Weiss (1990) — indexability + a global attractor of the mean-field ODE implies WIP is asymptotically optimal, plus an indexable counterexample (stable limit cycle) where WIP is NOT asymptotically optimal, so Whittle's conjecture is false in general (a technical gap patched by the 1991 addendum). (ii) Gast-Gaujal-Yan (2023) — under indexability + uniform global attractor + a non-degenerate fixed point, the gap is $O(e^{-cN})$; at a degenerate fixed point it can be only $O(1/\sqrt{N})$; with no global attractor WIP converges to a limit-cycle time-average and is generally suboptimal. (iii) Gast-Gaujal-Yan (Math of OR 2024) — necessary AND sufficient conditions for general LP/priority policies for each rate regime; an explicit LP-index policy is asymptotically optimal with $O(1/\sqrt{N})$ on ALL models, exponential under non-degeneracy + UGAP. (iv) Verloop (2016) gives asymptotic optimality of priority policies (incl. non-indexable) under a global attractor. (v) Hong-Xie-Chen-Wang removed the hard-to-verify assumptions: $O(1/\sqrt{N})$ without UGAP (FTVA 2023; unichain+aperiodicity 2025), and $O(e^{-CN})$ under aperiodic-unichain + non-degeneracy + local stability with a matching lower bound (two-set policy 2024). Indexability itself is hard to verify; sufficient conditions exist only for structured families (Niño-Mora's partial conservation laws). Still open: a practically checkable characterization of the global-attractor/UGAP condition; the exact minimal assumptions per rate regime (esp. for index/priority policies); and whether indexability plus something easily verifiable can guarantee asymptotic (let alone exponential) optimality of the Whittle index policy specifically.

where_posed.text: Conjecture originates in Whittle, 'Restless bandits: activity allocation in a changing world,' J. Appl. Prob. 25A:287-298, 1988; the global-attractor condition and counterexample are established in Weber & Weiss, 'On an index policy for restless bandits,' J. Appl. Prob. 27(3):637-648, 1990 (addendum 1991).

problems.title: Optimal policy for the Bayesian multi-armed bandit with switching costs
problems.short_statement: In the discounted Bayesian multi-armed bandit, the Gittins index is optimal; but once switching arms costs $c > 0$, no per-arm index rule can be optimal (Banks-Sundaram). Open: a clean characterization of the optimal policy structure (or a provably near-optimal simple policy) for the discounted MAB with switching costs.
problems.open_problem: Characterize the optimal policy for discounted Bayesian multi-armed bandits with positive switching costs, or give a tractable policy with provable optimality-gap guarantees.
problems.full_statement:
Consider $n$ independent arms; arm $i$ is a Markov reward process with state $x_i$. At each $t$ the controller plays one arm, receiving discounted reward with factor $ eta \in (0,1)$; the played arm evolves and the others stay frozen. Switching from arm $i$ to $j 
eq i$ incurs cost $c_{ij} \ge 0$ (canonical case $c_{ij} = c > 0$). The objective is to maximize $\mathbb{E} ig[\sum_{t=0}^{\infty}  eta^t r_{a_t}(x_{a_t}(t)) - \sum_{t}  eta^t c_{a_{t-1} a_t}\mathbf{1}\{a_t 
eq a_{t-1}\} ig]$. Without switching costs the Gittins index theorem says always play the arm of highest index, computed from that arm alone. Banks-Sundaram (1994) show that for $c>0$ there is NO assignment of indices $
u_i(x_i)$ depending only on arm $i$ such that 'play the largest index' is optimal: the Gittins decomposition fails. Asawa-Teneketzis (1996) prove a partial result: it is optimal to continue the current arm and only consider switching at certain stopping times governed by the incumbent's Gittins index and a candidate 'switching index' — but this is not a globally index-decomposable optimal policy. $$	extbf{Open:}\quad 	ext{characterize the optimal policy of the discounted MAB with switching cost } c>0,$$ or give a tractable policy with provable optimality-gap guarantees for general arm state spaces. The exact structure remains open and is believed PSPACE-hard in the worst case (cf. the restless generalization, Papadimitriou-Tsitsiklis 1999).
problems.known_results: Proven: With any positive switching cost the Gittins-index decomposition fails — no per-arm index policy is optimal in general (Banks-Sundaram 1994). Proven (partial structure): under an optimal policy one need only consider switching at stopping times attaining the incumbent's Gittins index or a candidate's 'switching index,' and an algorithm to compute that switching index exists (Asawa-Teneketzis 1996), but this does not give a global index policy. The general optimal-policy computation is widely regarded as intractable (Jun 2004 survey); the restless/Markov generalization is PSPACE-complete (Papadimitriou-Tsitsiklis 1999). Open: a clean structural characterization of the exact optimal policy for general arm state spaces, or a tractable policy with provable optimality-gap guarantees. (This Bayesian/discounted setting is distinct from the regret-minimization setting in the companion entry.)

where_posed.text: Stated/originated in Banks & Sundaram, 'Switching Costs and the Gittins Index,' Econometrica 62(3):687-694, 1994, which proves no index rule is optimal once switching is costly; intractability framed in Jun's (2004) survey.
