problems.title: Minimax and gap-dependent regret for bandits with switching costs
problems.short_statement: For regret minimization with a unit cost per arm switch, the adversarial minimax regret is order $T^{2/3}$ (vs $T^{1/2}$ without), and the stochastic switch-budget case has matching phase-transition bounds. Open: the exact instance-dependent (gap-dependent) optimal regret and tight dependence on general non-unit switching-cost structures.
problems.open_problem: Determine the exact gap-dependent regret and the tight minimax dependence for stochastic bandits with general switching-cost matrices.
problems.full_statement: An algorithm chooses $a_t \in [K]$ for $t=1,\dots,T$, observes only the played arm's reward (bandit feedback), and incurs a switching cost whenever $a_t \neq a_{t-1}$; total cost = regret-from-rewards + switching cost. With unit costs, $R_T = (\max_a \sum_t \mu_a - \mathbb{E}\sum_t \mu_{a_t}) + \mathbb{E}[\sum_{t=2}^T \mathbf{1}\{a_t \neq a_{t-1}\}]$. Adversarial: Dekel-Ding-Koren-Peres (2014) prove the minimax value is $\widetilde{\Theta}(T^{2/3})$ (vs $\Theta(\sqrt{KT})$ without switching costs) via a multi-scale random-walk lower bound. Stochastic switch-budget: Simchi-Levi-Xu (2019) cap the number of switches at $S$ and prove matching bounds exhibiting equal-length 'phases' in $S$ where optimal regret is constant within a phase and drops sharply between phases. $$\textbf{Open (residual):}\ \text{the exact gap-dependent optimal regret with switching cost } c, \text{ and the tight minimax dependence under general (non-unit, arm-pair-dependent) switching-cost matrices,}$$ including matching constants and the precise interpolation between $T^{1/2}$ (cost $\to 0$) and $T^{2/3}$ (unit cost) in the stochastic case.
problems.known_results: Proven: adversarial unit-switching-cost minimax regret is $\tilde{\Theta}(T^{2/3})$ (Dekel et al. 2014); for the stochastic problem with a fixed budget S on the number of switches, matching bounds with explicit phase transitions (Simchi-Levi-Xu 2019); best-of-both-worlds algorithms achieving $O(T^{2/3})$ adversarial and $O(\sum_i \log T / \Delta_i)$ stochastic simultaneously (Rouyer-Seldin-Cesa-Bianchi 2021). Open/residual: exact instance-dependent (gap-dependent) optimal constants for stochastic switching-cost regret, and tight characterization for general non-unit / arm-pair-dependent switching-cost matrices. This is a regret-minimization counterpart to the Bayesian/Gittins entry; the core minimax rates are resolved, so confidence that a clean fully-open question remains is moderate.

where_posed.text: Core minimax rate established in Dekel, Ding, Koren, Peres, 'Bandits with Switching Costs: T^{2/3} Regret,' STOC 2014; the residual gap-dependent and non-unit-cost questions are noted in the surrounding literature (Simchi-Levi-Xu 2019; Rouyer-Seldin-Cesa-Bianchi 2021).

problems.title: Exact optimality of the cμ/θ index rule for multiclass queues with abandonment
problems.short_statement: A server handles several customer classes that abandon while waiting. Without abandonment the $c\mu$ rule is exactly optimal; with abandonment the $c\mu/\theta$ rule is asymptotically optimal in heavy traffic. Open: whether $c\mu/\theta$ - or any static priority/index rule - is exactly optimal for the discounted or average-cost problem, and under what conditions.
problems.open_problem: Characterize when a static priority or index rule, such as $c\mu/\theta$, is exactly optimal for multiclass queues with abandonment.
problems.full_statement: Consider a multiclass $M/M/1{+}M$ queue (or $M/M/N{+}M$ many-server system) with classes $i=1,\dots,K$. Class-$i$ arrivals are Poisson at rate $\lambda_i$, service is exponential at rate $\mu_i$, each waiting class-$i$ customer abandons (reneges) independently at rate $\theta_i>0$, and holding incurs cost rate $c_i$ per class-$i$ customer. A (preemptive or non-preemptive) policy $\pi$ chooses which class to serve. The objective is $$ \min_{\pi}\; \limsup_{T\to\infty}\frac{1}{T}\,\mathbb{E}^{\pi}\!\left[\int_0^T \sum_{i=1}^K c_i Q_i(t)\,dt\right] \quad\text{(or discounted)}, $$ where $Q_i(t)$ is the number of class-$i$ customers. The $c\mu/\theta$ rule serves a customer of a class maximizing $c_i\mu_i/\theta_i$. \textbf{Open:} Is the $c\mu/\theta$ rule — or any static priority / index policy — the EXACT optimal policy (discounted or average cost), and if not, under what conditions on $(c_i,\mu_i,\theta_i)$ is a static priority rule exactly optimal? In contrast to the no-abandonment case ($\theta_i=0$), where the $c\mu$ rule (serve the class of largest $c_i\mu_i$) is provably exactly optimal, no exact index characterization is known when $\theta_i>0$, and the optimal policy is generally not a static priority rule.
problems.known_results: Proven: (i) Without abandonment (θ_i=0), the cμ rule is exactly optimal for the single-server multiclass problem (Cox & Smith; Buyukkoc-Varaiya-Walrand 1985). (ii) With abandonment, the cμ/θ rule is asymptotically optimal in the many-server Halfin-Whitt regime (Atar-Giat-Shimkin 2010) and under ergodic/average cost (Atar-Giat-Shimkin 2011). (iii) For the EXACT problem, Down-Koole-Lewis (2011) show the classic cμ-type priority rule does NOT hold in general with abandonment, but an additional condition on the ordering of the θ_i is sufficient to recover a static priority rule. (iv) Larrañaga-Ayesta-Verloop (2014, 2015) derive Whittle/index heuristics (via a restless-bandit relaxation) that are asymptotically optimal and perform well numerically but are not claimed exactly optimal. Open: a full characterization of the exact optimal policy for the multiclass M/M/1+M (and M/M/N+M) abandonment scheduling problem under discounted or average cost; in particular it is open in general (beyond the special orderings of Down-Koole-Lewis) when a static priority / index rule such as cμ/θ is exactly optimal.

where_posed.text: The cμ/θ rule and its heavy-traffic optimality are introduced in Atar, Giat, Shimkin, 'The cμ/θ Rule for Many-Server Queues with Abandonment,' Operations Research 58(5):1427-1439, 2010; the exact-optimality gap is delineated by Down-Koole-Lewis (2011) and the index-heuristic line by Larrañaga-Ayesta-Verloop (2015).

problems.title: Optimal dispatching to heterogeneous parallel servers (JSQ optimality fails)
problems.short_statement: For identical exponential servers, Join-the-Shortest-Queue is provably optimal. When servers have different service rates, JSQ is no longer optimal and no simple policy is known to exactly minimize mean response time. Characterizing the optimal dispatching policy for heterogeneous parallel servers is open.
problems.open_problem: Determine the exact optimal dispatching policy for heterogeneous parallel-server queues, especially beyond asymptotic or heuristic speed-aware rules.
problems.full_statement: Consider $N$ parallel single-server queues with a central dispatcher. Jobs arrive as a Poisson process of rate $\lambda$; upon arrival a job is irrevocably routed to one queue based on the observed queue-length vector $\mathbf{Q}(t)=(Q_1,\dots,Q_N)$ (full information). Server $j$ works at rate $\mu_j$ (exponential service), FCFS. \textbf{Homogeneous ($\mu_j\equiv\mu$):} JSQ — route to $\arg\min_j Q_j$ — stochastically minimizes $\mathbf{Q}(t)$ in an appropriate order and minimizes $\mathbb{E}\sum_j f(Q_j)$ for increasing convex $f$, hence mean response time (Winston 1977; Weber 1978). \textbf{Open (heterogeneous, $\mu_j$ distinct):} determine the policy minimizing long-run mean response time $$ \min_{\pi}\; \limsup_{T\to\infty} \frac{1}{T}\,\mathbb{E}^{\pi}\!\left[\int_0^T \sum_{j=1}^N Q_j(t)\,dt\right] $$ (equivalently mean sojourn time by Little's law). JSQ is provably NOT optimal when the $\mu_j$ differ; the exact optimal routing policy is unknown and conjectured to have a complicated, non-index threshold structure. A clean stated-open variant: is JSQ (or any explicit policy) optimal for minimizing mean response time with GENERAL (non-exponential) i.i.d. service even for identical servers?
problems.known_results: Proven: for homogeneous exponential servers, JSQ stochastically minimizes the sorted queue-length process and minimizes E[sum f($Q_j$)] for increasing-convex f, hence mean response time (Winston 1977; Weber 1978 extends to service times with non-decreasing hazard rate). Mean-field/asymptotic: power-of-d gives the double-exponential tail improvement over random routing (Vvedenskaya-Dobrushin-Karpelevich 1996; Mitzenmacher 2001); JSQ is heavy-traffic optimal with many servers in the Halfin-Whitt regime (Eschenfeldt-Gamarnik 2018). Heterogeneous servers: JSQ is provably NOT optimal once rates differ; SED/JSED and speed-aware variants are asymptotically (heavy-traffic) optimal in several settings, but no policy is known to be exactly optimal for mean response time, and the optimal policy is believed to have a complicated threshold structure. Open: (a) the exact optimal dispatching policy for heterogeneous parallel servers (distinct μ_j), explicitly noted as open in the load-balancing literature and the SIAM Review survey (van der Boor et al. 2022); (b) even for identical servers, exact non-asymptotic optimality of JSQ for mean sojourn time under general service-time distributions is open.

where_posed.text: JSQ optimality for identical servers is established in Winston, 'Optimality of the shortest line discipline,' J. Appl. Prob. 14(1):181-189, 1977 (and Weber 1978); the heterogeneous-server open problem is highlighted in the load-balancing literature and the van der Boor et al. (2022) SIAM Review survey.

problems.title: Tight regret of LP-relaxation policies for network revenue management: degenerate and choice-based cases
problems.short_statement: In network revenue management, the deterministic LP upper-bounds optimal revenue and re-solving heuristics achieve $O(1)$ regret under non-degeneracy in the independent-demand case. For choice-based NRM, no policy is known to achieve constant ($T$-independent) regret without a non-degeneracy assumption, and the tight relaxation gap and best simple-policy regret are uncharacterized.
problems.open_problem: Find a tractable policy with $O(1)$ regret for general choice-based network revenue management, including degenerate CDLP instances, or prove such regret is impossible.
problems.full_statement: Quantity-based NRM: $T$ periods, $m$ resources with integer capacities $c \in \mathbb{Z}_{+}^m$, $n$ products; product $j$ consumes bundle $A_{\cdot j} \in \{0,1\}^m$ and yields revenue $r_j$; demand is stochastic. In the choice-based model, at each period the firm offers an assortment $S \subseteq \{1,\dots,n\}$ and an arriving customer chooses $j \in S$ with probability $P_j(S)$ (e.g. MNL). Let $V^*$ be the optimal DP value and $V^{\mathrm{CDLP}} = \max_{\lambda \geq 0} \sum_S \lambda_S (\sum_j P_j(S) r_j)$ s.t. $\sum_S \lambda_S \sum_j P_j(S) A_{ij} \leq c_i\ \forall i$, $\sum_S \lambda_S \leq T$, so $V^{\mathrm{CDLP}} \geq V^*$ (the choice-based deterministic LP). Define the regret of policy $\pi$ as $V^* - V^\pi$ under fluid scaling $c \to k c$, $T \to kT$, $k \to \infty$. $$\textbf{Open: } \text{Is there a tractable policy } \pi \text{ (e.g. CDLP-based re-solving/decomposition) with regret } O(1) \text{ (independent of } k,T\text{) for } \textit{all} \text{ choice-based instances, including degenerate CDLP?}$$ Equivalently, characterize $\inf_\pi \sup_{\text{instances}} \mathrm{Regret}(\pi)$ and the tightest bound on $V^{\mathrm{CDLP}} - V^*$ as a function of $m,n,T$ without a non-degeneracy assumption. For independent demand, re-solving achieves O(1) regret under non-degeneracy, and recent work removes non-degeneracy for continuous-value models.
problems.known_results: Independent-demand quantity-based NRM: Talluri & van Ryzin (1998) proved DLP bid-price controls are asymptotically optimal; naive frequent re-solving incurs $O(\sqrt{T})$ loss. Jasin & Kumar (2012) gave a re-solving heuristic with $O(1)$ loss under non-degeneracy. Bumpensanti & Wang (2020) achieve uniformly bounded $O(1)$ regret independent of T and capacities via infrequent re-solving with probability thresholding, under non-degeneracy. Vera & Banerjee (2021) obtain constant regret for online allocation with finite types. For degenerate LP / continuous-value models, Jiang-Ma-Zhang (2025) prove $O(\log ^2 T)$ regret with only densities bounded away from 0 ($O(\log T)$ under a growth condition), removing non-degeneracy for that formulation; whether $O(1)$ is achievable for general degenerate discrete instances is unsettled. Choice-based NRM: Liu & van Ryzin (2008) showed the CDLP and its decomposition heuristic are asymptotically optimal as demand/capacity scale; Jasin & Kumar (2012) handle choice but rely on non-degeneracy. The non-linear structure of choice makes the fluid relaxation non-linear, and no policy is currently proven to achieve constant (T-independent) regret for general/degenerate choice-based instances, nor is the tight relaxation gap characterized — the genuinely open piece as of 2024-2025.

where_posed.text: The O(1)-regret re-solving result (independent demand, non-degenerate) is in Bumpensanti & Wang, 'A Re-Solving Heuristic with Uniformly Bounded Loss for Network Revenue Management,' Management Science 66(7):2993-3009, 2020; the residual choice-based/degenerate open question is framed in the surrounding literature (Liu-van Ryzin 2008; Jiang-Ma-Zhang 2025).

problems.title: Optimal policy for stochastic single-machine weighted completion time with release dates or precedence
problems.short_statement: On a single machine with random processing times, WSEPT is optimal for minimizing expected total weighted completion time with no release dates or precedence (Rothkopf 1966). With release dates and/or precedence, the optimal non-anticipative policy is uncharacterized even for exponential jobs; only constant-factor LP-based approximations are known.
problems.open_problem: Characterize the optimal non-anticipative policy for stochastic scheduling with release dates or precedence constraints, or determine the precise complexity of computing it.
problems.full_statement: Single machine, jobs $j = 1,\dots,n$ with independent random processing times $P_j$ and weights $w_j > 0$; $C_j$ is the policy-dependent random completion time. A policy $\pi$ must be non-anticipative (decisions at time $t$ depend only on information available by $t$). Objective: $$\min_{\pi \in \Pi_{\mathrm{non\text{-}ant}}} \ \mathbb{E}\Big[\sum_{j=1}^n w_j C_j^{\pi}\Big].$$ Baseline (Rothkopf 1966): with all $r_j = 0$ and no precedence, the static list policy WSEPT (process in non-increasing order of $w_j / \mathbb{E}[P_j]$) is optimal. $$\textbf{Open (release dates): } \text{with release dates } r_j \ (1\,|\,r_j\,|\,\mathbb{E}\sum w_j C_j), \text{ characterize an optimal non-anticipative policy, even when } P_j \sim \mathrm{Exp}(\mu_j).$$ $$\textbf{Open (precedence): } \text{with precedence constraints (e.g. series-parallel) and } P_j \sim \mathrm{Exp}(\mu_j), \text{ characterize the optimal policy for } 1\,|\,\mathrm{prec}\,|\,\mathbb{E}\sum w_j C_j.$$ No closed-form optimal priority policy is known in either case; the gap between best-known approximation guarantees and optimality is open. (The deterministic special cases $1\,|\,r_j\,|\,\sum w_j C_j$ and $1\,|\,\mathrm{prec}\,|\,\sum w_j C_j$ are already strongly NP-hard.)
problems.known_results: Rothkopf (1966) proved WSEPT is optimal for the single machine with no release dates and no precedence. For exponential processing times, WSEPT/cµ-type rules are optimal in several special structures, but with general release dates or precedence the optimal non-anticipative policy is uncharacterized even for exponential jobs. Best positive results are LP-based approximations: Möhring-Schulz-Uetz (1999, JACM) gave the first constant-factor guarantees for LP-guided priority policies with release dates and/or precedence; later work (Skutella-Sviridenko-Uetz; Correa-Schulz) improved constants and extended to unrelated machines, but these only bound the ratio to an optimal policy, not identify it. On complexity: the deterministic versions 1|$r_j$|sum $w_j C_j$ and 1|prec|sum $w_j C_j$ are strongly NP-hard; for the genuinely stochastic problem a 2026 result (arXiv:2601.17425) shows minimizing E[sum $w_j C_j$] on parallel machines is #P-hard even for two-point distributions with unit weights (and even evaluating WSEPT's expected cost is #P-hard) — the first hardness not inherited from the deterministic counterpart. The exact optimal-policy structure for the single-machine release-date and precedence cases, and the precise complexity of the single-machine stochastic version, remain open.

where_posed.text: The WSEPT optimality baseline is Rothkopf, 'Scheduling with Random Service Times,' Management Science 12(9):707-713, 1966; the open release-date and precedence cases (with only LP-based approximations known) are developed in Möhring-Schulz-Uetz (1999, JACM) and the subsequent stochastic-scheduling literature.

problems.title: The 4/3 Conjecture for the Subtour LP Integrality Gap (Metric TSP)
problems.short_statement: For metric TSP, the integrality gap of the subtour-elimination (Held-Karp) LP relaxation is conjectured to be exactly $4/3$. Examples force it to be at least $4/3$, but the best proven upper bound is only barely below $3/2$.
problems.open_problem: Determine the exact integrality gap of the subtour-elimination (Held-Karp) LP for metric TSP; in particular, prove or refute the $4/3$ conjecture.
problems.full_statement: For the symmetric Traveling Salesman Problem with the triangle inequality, the standard linear-programming relaxation (the subtour-elimination / Held-Karp LP) gives a lower bound on the optimal tour length. Let $\mathrm{OPT}(I)$ be the optimal tour length of a metric TSP instance $I$ and $\mathrm{LP}(I)$ the optimal value of the subtour-elimination (Held-Karp) relaxation. Define the integrality gap $\gamma = \sup_I \frac{\mathrm{OPT}(I)}{\mathrm{LP}(I)}$ (the worst-case ratio of the true optimum to the LP bound). \textbf{Conjecture:} $\gamma = \tfrac{4}{3}$. Known: $\tfrac{4}{3} \le \gamma \le \tfrac{3}{2} - \varepsilon$ for some $\varepsilon > 10^{-36}$. Determine $\gamma$ exactly.
problems.known_results: The lower bound $4/3$ is witnessed by standard families of metric TSP instances, so the conjectured value cannot be improved. For roughly forty years the best general upper bound was $3/2$; Karlin, Klein, and Oveis Gharan broke this barrier by proving an upper bound of $3/2-\epsilon$ for an extremely small absolute constant $\epsilon$. The resulting gap is conceptually important: the lower bound is exactly the conjectured answer, while the best proof technique still sits very close to $3/2$.

where_posed.text: A long-standing conjecture in combinatorial optimization; surveyed and stated in Traub & Vygen, 'Approximation Algorithms for Traveling Salesman Problems' (Cambridge University Press, 2024).

source.title: Approximation Algorithms for Traveling Salesman Problems

problems.title: Exact Integrality Gap of the Held-Karp LP for Asymmetric TSP
problems.short_statement: For asymmetric metric TSP, the Held-Karp LP integrality gap is now known to be a constant, but the exact value is open: known examples force it to be at least $2$, while the best proof only shows it is at most $22$.
problems.open_problem: Determine the exact integrality gap of the Held-Karp LP for asymmetric metric TSP, closing the gap between the lower bound $2$ and the upper bound $22$.
problems.full_statement: In asymmetric TSP, the distance from A to B need not equal the distance from B to A (but the triangle inequality still holds). The Held-Karp LP lower-bounds the optimal tour. For asymmetric metric TSP, let $\gamma = \sup_I \frac{\mathrm{OPT}(I)}{\mathrm{LP}(I)}$ be the integrality gap of the Held-Karp relaxation, where $\mathrm{OPT}(I)$ is the optimal tour length and $\mathrm{LP}(I)$ the LP optimum on instance $I$. Known: $2 \le \gamma \le 22$. \textbf{Determine $\gamma$ exactly.} It is widely believed $\gamma$ is much smaller than 22.
problems.known_results: Charikar, Goemans, and Karloff gave instances showing that the Held-Karp relaxation has integrality gap at least $2$. Svensson, Tarnawski, and Vegh proved the first constant upper bound, $319$, resolving the earlier question of whether the gap was bounded at all. Traub and Vygen later improved the upper bound to $22$. The exact value is still unknown, and the large separation between $2$ and $22$ suggests that either sharper rounding or stronger lower-bound constructions are needed.

where_posed.text: The constant-factor upper bound (and the open exact-value question) is established in Svensson, Tarnawski, Vegh, 'A Constant-Factor Approximation Algorithm for the Asymmetric Traveling Salesman Problem,' JACM (STOC 2018); lower bound 2 from Charikar-Goemans-Karloff (2006).

problems.title: Integrality Gap of the Bidirected-Cut Relaxation for Steiner Tree
problems.short_statement: For the Steiner tree problem, the integrality gap of the bidirected-cut relaxation is conjectured to be close to $1$; the best known lower bound is $6/5 = 1.2$ and the best upper bound is $1.9988$ (only shown below $2$ in 2024).
problems.open_problem: Determine the exact integrality gap of the bidirected-cut relaxation for Steiner tree.
problems.full_statement: In the Steiner tree problem you must connect a set of required terminals in a weighted graph as cheaply as possible, possibly using extra (Steiner) vertices. The bidirected-cut relaxation is a natural LP that points each edge in both directions. For the Steiner tree problem, let $\gamma_{\mathrm{BCR}}$ be the integrality gap of the bidirected-cut relaxation (the worst-case ratio of the optimal integral Steiner tree cost to the LP optimum). Known: $\tfrac{6}{5} = 1.2 \le \gamma_{\mathrm{BCR}} \le 1.9988$. \textbf{Determine $\gamma_{\mathrm{BCR}}$;} it is conjectured to be substantially below 2.
problems.known_results: Lower bound $6/5$ = 1.2 (Vicari 2020), improving the older $36/31$. Upper bound 1.9988 (Byrka-Grandoni-Traub, FOCS 2024); before 2024 it was unknown whether the gap beat the trivial 2. A 2026 preprint claims ~1.898. Conjectured value much smaller.

where_posed.text: The first sub-2 upper bound (and the open exact-value question) is in Byrka, Grandoni, Traub, 'The Bidirected Cut Relaxation for Steiner Tree has Integrality Gap Smaller than 2,' FOCS 2024; lower bound 6/5 from Vicari (2020).

problems.title: Best Approximation Ratio for Steiner Tree (1.39 vs. hardness)
problems.short_statement: For graph Steiner tree, the best polynomial-time algorithm achieves ratio $\ln 4 \approx 1.39$, while the best hardness only rules out ratios below $96/95 \approx 1.01$. Closing this gap - and pinning down the hypergraphic-LP integrality gap - is open.
problems.open_problem: Determine the optimal polynomial-time approximation ratio for graph Steiner tree and the exact integrality gap of the hypergraphic LP.
problems.full_statement: For the graph Steiner tree problem (connect a set of required terminals in a weighted graph as cheaply as possible, possibly using extra Steiner vertices), the best known polynomial-time algorithm achieves a ratio of $\ln 4 \approx 1.39$ via iterative randomized rounding of a stronger 'hypergraphic' LP. \textbf{Determine the best polynomial-time approximation ratio $\rho$ for graph Steiner tree (assuming $\mathrm{P} \ne \mathrm{NP}$).} Known: $\tfrac{96}{95} \approx 1.0105 \le \rho \le \ln 4 \approx 1.386$. Relatedly, the hypergraphic LP integrality gap satisfies $\tfrac{8}{7} \le \gamma \le \ln 4$ in general graphs.
problems.known_results: The best polynomial-time approximation ratio is $\ln 4 \approx 1.386$, achieved by the iterative-randomized-rounding algorithm of Byrka, Grandoni, Rothvoss, and Sanita for the hypergraphic relaxation. The strongest NP-hardness threshold is only $96/95 \approx 1.0105$, due to Chlebik and Chlebikova. For the hypergraphic LP itself, known instances give an integrality gap of at least $8/7$, while the rounding analysis gives an upper bound of $\ln 4$. The open problem is to determine the true approximability threshold for graph Steiner tree and, relatedly, the exact integrality gap of the hypergraphic LP.

where_posed.text: The ln 4 algorithm and hypergraphic LP are from Byrka, Grandoni, Rothvoss, Sanita, 'Steiner Tree Approximation via Iterative Randomized Rounding,' JACM (STOC 2010); the hardness bound 96/95 from Chlebik-Chlebikova (2008). The gap-closing is a well-known open problem.

problems.title: Integrality Gap of the Configuration LP for Restricted Santa Claus
problems.short_statement: In restricted Santa Claus (max-min fair allocation), the configuration-LP integrality gap is known to be a constant between $2$ and about $3.81$; determining it exactly is a long-standing open problem.
problems.open_problem: Determine the exact integrality gap of the configuration LP for restricted Santa Claus / restricted max-min fair allocation.
problems.full_statement: In the restricted Santa Claus (max-min fair allocation) problem, indivisible gifts must be handed to children so as to maximize the happiness of the least-happy child, where each gift has a fixed value and can only go to children who want it. The configuration LP is the standard relaxation. For the restricted max-min allocation (restricted Santa Claus) problem, let $\gamma$ be the integrality gap of the configuration LP. Known: $2 \le \gamma \le 3 + \tfrac{21}{26} \approx 3.808$. The upper bound is non-constructive (via local search whose polynomial-time convergence is itself open). \textbf{Determine $\gamma$.}
problems.known_results: Bansal and Sviridenko introduced the configuration LP for the Santa Claus problem, and Feige's hypergraph-matching framework showed that the restricted case has a constant integrality gap. The best current upper bound is $3+21/26 \approx 3.808$, due to Cheng and Mao, while known examples force a gap of at least $2$. The open problem is to determine the exact integrality gap of this LP. This is not just a matter of improving constants: it asks for the true strength of the standard relaxation for restricted max-min allocation.

where_posed.text: The constant-gap result for restricted Santa Claus traces to Asadpour, Feige, Saberi, 'Santa Claus Meets Hypergraph Matchings,' ACM Trans. Algorithms (APPROX 2008); best upper bound 3+21/26 from Cheng-Mao (2022). The exact gap is a recognized open problem.

problems.title: Beating the 2-Approximation for Makespan on Unrelated Machines (R||Cmax)
problems.short_statement: For makespan minimization on unrelated machines, the classic Lenstra-Shmoys-Tardos algorithm gives a $2$-approximation and it is NP-hard to beat $3/2$. Whether a ratio below $2$ (ideally $3/2$) is achievable has been open since 1990.
problems.open_problem: For $R \,||\, C_{\max}$ (minimize makespan on unrelated parallel machines), determine the best polynomial-time approximation ratio $\rho$.
problems.full_statement: Given jobs and machines where each job takes a different (machine-dependent) time on each machine, assign every job to a machine to minimize the makespan (the load of the busiest machine). For $R \,||\, C_{\max}$ (minimize makespan on unrelated parallel machines), \textbf{determine the best polynomial-time approximation ratio $\rho$.} Known: $\tfrac{3}{2} \le \rho \le 2$ (both bounds from Lenstra-Shmoys-Tardos). Can $\rho < 2$? Is $\rho = \tfrac{3}{2}$ achievable?
problems.known_results: Lenstra, Shmoys, and Tardos gave the classic $2$-approximation and also proved that no polynomial-time algorithm can achieve a ratio below $3/2$ unless $\mathrm{P}=\mathrm{NP}$. Several structured special cases, including restricted assignment and graph balancing, admit better guarantees. For the full unrelated-machines problem, however, no polynomial-time approximation ratio strictly below $2$ is known. The open problem is to beat the $2$ barrier, or to prove a stronger hardness result that explains why the original $[3/2,2]$ gap persists.

where_posed.text: Both the 2-approximation and the 3/2 hardness are from Lenstra, Shmoys, Tardos, 'Approximation algorithms for scheduling unrelated parallel machines,' Mathematical Programming, 1990; closing the gap is a classic open problem.

problems.title: Beck-Fiala Conjecture (Discrepancy of Bounded-Degree Set Systems)
problems.short_statement: Color elements of a set system red/blue to minimize the worst set imbalance (discrepancy). If every element lies in at most $t$ sets, the Beck-Fiala theorem gives at most $2t-1$; the conjecture is that the truth is only $O(\sqrt{t})$, independent of the number of sets or elements.
problems.open_problem: Prove or refute the Beck-Fiala conjecture that every degree-$t$ set system has discrepancy $O(\sqrt t)$.
problems.full_statement: Color the elements of a set system red and blue so that every set is as balanced as possible; the discrepancy is the worst imbalance over all sets. Let $\mathcal{S}$ be a set system in which every element belongs to at most $t$ sets (equivalently, a matrix $A \in \{0,1\}^{m \times n}$ with at most $t$ ones per column). \textbf{Conjecture (Beck-Fiala):} $\mathrm{disc}(\mathcal{S}) = \min_{x \in \{\pm 1\}^n}\|Ax\|_\infty = O(\sqrt{t})$, independent of $m$ and $n$. The Beck-Fiala theorem guarantees discrepancy at most $2t-1$.
problems.known_results: Beck and Fiala proved the original $2t-1$ discrepancy bound, and Bukh later improved this slightly to $2t-O(\log^* t)$. A different line of work based on Banaszczyk's vector-balancing theorem gives $O(\sqrt{t\log n})$, which has the conjectured dependence on $t$ but still depends on the number of elements. Bansal and Jiang prove the conjectured $O(\sqrt{t})$ bound in the regime $t\ge \log^2 n$. The remaining open case is the full, dimension-free $O(\sqrt{t})$ bound for every $t$.

where_posed.text: Conjectured in Beck & Fiala, 'Integer-making theorems,' Discrete Applied Mathematics, 1981, which also proves the 2t-1 bound.

source.title: Integer-making theorems

problems.title: Komlos Conjecture (O(1) Discrepancy of Unit-Norm Vectors)
problems.short_statement: Given any vectors of Euclidean length at most $1$, the Komlos conjecture says one can sign them so every coordinate of the signed sum stays bounded by a universal constant, independent of dimension or count. It generalizes the Beck-Fiala conjecture.
problems.open_problem: Prove or refute the Komlos conjecture that unit Euclidean norm vectors can always be signed to have $O(1)$ discrepancy in every coordinate.
problems.full_statement: Given any collection of vectors each of Euclidean length at most 1, the Komlos conjecture says you can attach a $+$ or $-$ sign to each so that every coordinate of the signed sum stays bounded by a universal constant — no matter how many vectors there are or what dimension they live in. \textbf{Conjecture (Komlos):} there is a universal constant $K$ such that for any vectors $v_1,\dots,v_n \in \mathbb{R}^m$ with $\|v_i\|_2 \le 1$, there exist signs $\varepsilon_i \in \{\pm 1\}$ with $\big\|\sum_i \varepsilon_i v_i\big\|_\infty \le K = O(1)$, independent of $m$ and $n$. This generalizes the Beck-Fiala conjecture.
problems.known_results: The conjecture predicts a universal $O(1)$ bound, independent of both the dimension and the number of vectors. Banaszczyk's theorem gives the best classical general bound, $O(\sqrt{\log n})$, and Bansal, Dadush, and Garg made this algorithmic. Bansal and Jiang recently improved the dependence to $O((\log n)^{1/4})$. The best known lower bound on the universal constant is about $2.414$, and a proof of Komlos would imply the Beck-Fiala conjecture.

where_posed.text: A classical conjecture in discrepancy theory; stated in Nikolov's Prague Summer School lecture notes on Discrepancy Theory (2018).

source.title: Prague Summer School: Discrepancy Theory (Lecture notes)

additional_refs.authors: Nikhil Bansal, Daniel Dadush, Shashwat Garg
additional_refs.title: An Algorithm for Komlos Conjecture Matching Banaszczyk's Bound

problems.title: Matrix Spencer Conjecture
problems.short_statement: The matrix version of Spencer's 'six standard deviations' theorem asks: given $n$ symmetric $n\times n$ matrices of operator norm at most $1$, can one choose signs so the signed sum has spectral norm $O(\sqrt{n})$? Proven for low rank; open in general.
problems.open_problem: Prove or refute the full-rank Matrix Spencer conjecture: can $n$ bounded symmetric matrices always be signed to have spectral norm $O(\sqrt n)$?
problems.full_statement: Spencer's classic 'six standard deviations' theorem balances n sets on n elements with discrepancy O(sqrt(n)); the matrix version asks the same for matrices. \textbf{Conjecture (Matrix Spencer):} for symmetric matrices $A_1,\dots,A_n \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ with $\|A_i\|_{\mathrm{op}} \le 1$, there exist signs $x_i \in \{\pm 1\}$ with $\big\|\sum_i x_i A_i\big\|_{\mathrm{op}} = O(\sqrt{n})$. (The diagonal case is Spencer's theorem.) Proven for rank $\le n/\log^3 n$; open in general (the full-rank case).
problems.known_results: The conjectured bound is $O(\sqrt n)$, matching the scale of Spencer's original set-system theorem in the diagonal case. Bansal, Jiang, and Meka proved this bound for matrices of rank at most $n/\log^3 n$, which covers a large low-rank regime and introduced new matrix-discrepancy techniques. The main unresolved case is full rank, where the noncommutative nature of the matrices prevents a direct extension of scalar discrepancy arguments.

where_posed.text: The poly-logarithmic-rank resolution (and statement of the general open conjecture) is in Bansal, Jiang, Meka, 'Resolving Matrix Spencer Conjecture Up to Poly-logarithmic Rank,' STOC 2023 (SIAM J. Computing 2024).

source.authors: Nikhil Bansal, Haotian Jiang, Raghu Meka
source.title: Resolving Matrix Spencer Conjecture Up to Poly-logarithmic Rank

problems.title: Approximability of Unit-Demand Capacitated Vehicle Routing
problems.short_statement: In capacitated vehicle routing, the classic iterated tour partitioning gives ratio $\alpha+1$ ($\alpha$ = best TSP ratio). A 2021 breakthrough shaved off a tiny amount; whether the $+1$ can be reduced - ideally to match TSP - is open.
problems.open_problem: can $\rho$ be pushed toward $\alpha + o(1)$, and is there CVRP-specific hardness beyond the TSP-inherited bound?
problems.full_statement: In capacitated vehicle routing, vehicles of capacity $Q$ based at a depot must together visit all customers (each with unit demand) using minimum total travel distance. For unit-demand (and splittable) metric CVRP, let $\rho$ be the best polynomial-time approximation ratio and $\alpha$ the best metric-TSP ratio. Classic iterated tour partitioning gives $\rho \le \alpha + 1$. Blauth-Traub-Vygen improved this to $\rho \le \alpha + 1 - \varepsilon$ with $\varepsilon > 1/3000$. \textbf{Open:} can $\rho$ be pushed toward $\alpha + o(1)$, and is there CVRP-specific hardness beyond the TSP-inherited bound?
problems.known_results: Iterated tour partitioning (Haimovich-Rinnooy Kan 1985) gives $\alpha$+1. Improved to $\alpha$+1-$\epsilon$, $\epsilon$>$1/3000$ (Blauth-Traub-Vygen 2021/2023) — first improvement in ~35 years. Hardness only TSP-inherited. Exact approximability open.

where_posed.text: The first improvement over alpha+1 (and the open question of pushing toward alpha) is in Blauth, Traub, Vygen, 'Improving the Approximation Ratio for Capacitated Vehicle Routing,' Mathematical Programming (IPCO 2021); the alpha+1 baseline from Haimovich-Rinnooy Kan (1985).

source.title: Improving the Approximation Ratio for Capacitated Vehicle Routing

problems.title: Metric Uncapacitated Facility Location: 1.463 vs. 1.488
problems.short_statement: For metric uncapacitated facility location, the best known algorithm achieves $1.488$ while it is NP-hard to beat $1.463$. This $\approx 0.025$ gap has stood for over a decade and is one of the cleanest open problems in approximation.
problems.open_problem: Determine the exact polynomial-time approximability threshold for metric uncapacitated facility location between the $1.463$ hardness and the $1.488$ algorithm.
problems.full_statement: Open some facilities (paying an opening cost) and connect each client to an open facility (paying the distance), minimizing the total. For metric uncapacitated facility location, \textbf{determine the best polynomial-time approximation ratio $\rho$ (assuming $\mathrm{P} \ne \mathrm{NP}$).} Known: $1.463 \le \rho \le 1.488$. The folklore belief is $\rho = 1.463$.
problems.known_results: Guha and Khuller constructed hardness examples near $1.463$, later strengthened to a standard $\mathrm{P}\ne\mathrm{NP}$ hardness statement by Sviridenko. Shi Li's 2013 algorithm achieves approximation ratio $1.488$, and no better general ratio is currently known. The open problem is to close this narrow $0.025$ window and determine the exact approximability threshold for metric uncapacitated facility location.

where_posed.text: The 1.488 algorithm is from Shi Li, 'A 1.488 Approximation Algorithm for the Uncapacitated Facility Location Problem,' Information and Computation (ICALP 2011); the 1.463 hardness from Guha-Khuller (strengthened by Sviridenko). The gap is a recognized open problem.

problems.title: Metric k-Median: 1.736 Hardness vs. 2+epsilon Algorithm
problems.short_statement: For metric $k$-median, it is NP-hard to approximate better than $1 + 2/e \approx 1.736$, and as of 2025 the best algorithm achieves $2 + \epsilon$. Determining the exact approximability in $[1.736, 2]$ is a major open problem.
problems.open_problem: The natural LP relaxation has integrality gap at least $2 - o(1)$, so the remaining question is whether the true threshold is below, at, or above the LP barrier near $2$.
problems.full_statement: In metric $k$-median, one is given a finite metric space $(V,d)$, a set of demand points (clients) in $V$, and an integer $k$. The algorithm must choose at most $k$ centers from $V$ and assign each client to its nearest chosen center, minimizing the total connection cost $\sum_j d(j,S)$, where $S$ is the selected center set. The open problem is to determine the best possible polynomial-time approximation ratio $\rho$ for this model. Known bounds are $1 + \tfrac{2}{e} \approx 1.736 \le \rho \le 2 + \varepsilon$. The natural LP relaxation has integrality gap at least $2 - o(1)$, so the remaining question is whether the true threshold is below, at, or above the LP barrier near $2$.
problems.known_results: Jain, Mahdian, and Saberi proved that approximating metric $k$-median below $1+2/e \approx 1.736$ is NP-hard. The best current algorithm achieves $2+\epsilon$, due to Cohen-Addad, Grandoni, Lee, Schwiegelshohn, and Svensson, improving the long-standing $2.613$ barrier. The natural LP relaxation has integrality gap at least $2-o(1)$. The open problem is to determine whether the true threshold is near the hardness bound, near $2$, or somewhere in between; closing it likely requires either a different relaxation/algorithmic idea or a stronger hardness construction.

where_posed.text: The (2+epsilon) algorithm is from Cohen-Addad, Grandoni, Lee, Schwiegelshohn, Svensson, 'A (2+epsilon)-Approximation Algorithm for Metric k-Median,' STOC 2025; the 1.736 hardness from Jain-Mahdian-Saberi (2002). The gap is a major open problem.

source.authors: Vincent Cohen-Addad, Fabrizio Grandoni, Euiwoong Lee, Chris Schwiegelshohn, Ola Svensson
source.title: A (2+epsilon)-Approximation Algorithm for Metric k-Median

problems.title: Graph Balancing (Restricted Two-Machine Makespan)
problems.short_statement: Graph balancing is unrelated-machine scheduling where each job can go on only two machines (same time on either). It is NP-hard to beat $3/2$ and the best ratio is below $1.75$, but the exact threshold is open.
problems.open_problem: Determine the best polynomial-time approximation ratio $\rho$.
problems.full_statement: Graph balancing is the special case of unrelated-machine scheduling where each job can go on only two machines (and takes the same time on either); equivalently, orient the edges of a weighted multigraph to minimize the maximum weighted in-degree. In graph balancing, each job $j$ has a single size $p_j$ and may be assigned to one of (at most) two machines. \textbf{Determine the best polynomial-time approximation ratio $\rho$.} Known: $\tfrac{3}{2} \le \rho < \tfrac{7}{4}$.
problems.known_results: Ebenlendr, Krcal, and Sgall proved $3/2$ hardness and gave a $7/4$-approximation, establishing graph balancing as one of the cleanest restricted-assignment gaps. Jansen and Rohwedder later used local search to beat the $7/4$ barrier. The open problem is to determine the exact approximation threshold: the lower bound remains $3/2$, while the best algorithm is only known to be some constant strictly below $1.75$.

where_posed.text: The 3/2 hardness and 7/4 algorithm are from Ebenlendr, Krcal, Sgall, 'Graph Balancing: A Special Case of Scheduling Unrelated Parallel Machines,' Algorithmica (SODA 2008); local search below 1.75 from Jansen-Rohwedder (2019). The exact ratio is open.

source.title: Graph Balancing: A Special Case of Scheduling Unrelated Parallel Machines

problems.title: Restricted Assignment Makespan (Polynomial-Time Below 2)
problems.short_statement: In restricted-assignment makespan, each job has a fixed size and a set of allowed machines. The general $2$-approximation applies and the configuration LP estimates the optimum within $\approx 1.94$, but producing an actual schedule below $2$ in polynomial time is open.
problems.open_problem: A polynomial-time schedule with ratio below 2 is open.
problems.full_statement: This is the special case of unrelated-machine makespan where each job has a fixed size and a set of machines it is allowed to run on (running time is the same on any allowed machine, infinite elsewhere). For restricted assignment ($p_{ij} \in \{p_j, \infty\}$), \textbf{determine the best polynomial-time approximation ratio $\rho$.} Known: $\tfrac{3}{2} \le \rho \le 2$. Svensson proved the configuration-LP integrality gap is $\le \tfrac{33}{17} \approx 1.94$ (estimation only, i.e. a bound on the optimum, not an actual schedule); Jansen-Rohwedder give a constructive $\tfrac{11}{6}$ in quasi-polynomial time. A polynomial-time schedule with ratio below 2 is open.
problems.known_results: The Lenstra-Shmoys-Tardos framework gives a $2$-approximation, and the problem is NP-hard to approximate below $3/2$. Svensson showed that the configuration LP has integrality gap at most $33/17\approx 1.94$, but this is an estimation result and does not directly produce a polynomial-time schedule. Jansen and Rohwedder later gave a constructive $11/6\approx 1.833$ approximation in quasi-polynomial time. The open problem is to find a genuinely polynomial-time algorithm with ratio strictly below $2$, or to prove that such a schedule cannot be efficiently constructed.

where_posed.text: The 1.94 configuration-LP estimation is from Svensson, 'Santa Claus Schedules Jobs on Unrelated Machines,' SIAM J. Computing (STOC 2011); quasi-polynomial 11/6 from Jansen-Rohwedder (2017). A polynomial-time schedule below 2 is a recognized open problem.

source.title: Santa Claus Schedules Jobs on Unrelated Machines

problems.title: Best Polynomial-Time Approximation for Restricted Santa Claus
problems.short_statement: Separately from the integrality-gap question, the best polynomial-time approximation ratio for restricted max-min fair allocation is open: non-constructive arguments give $\approx 3.8\text{--}4$, but the best actual polynomial-time algorithm only guarantees $4 + \delta$.
problems.open_problem: Determine the best polynomial-time approximation ratio $\rho$.
problems.full_statement: Separately from the integrality-gap question, there is the algorithmic question: what is the best ratio achievable in polynomial time for restricted max-min fair allocation (restricted Santa Claus)? \textbf{Determine the best polynomial-time approximation ratio $\rho$.} Known: $\rho \le 4 + \delta$ for any constant $\delta > 0$ (Cheng-Mao). The configuration LP certifies that allocations within a factor $\le 3.808$ of optimal exist (non-constructively), so closing the constructive vs. existential gap is open.
problems.known_results: Cheng and Mao give the best polynomial-time approximation, with ratio $4+\delta$ for any fixed $\delta>0$, improving earlier guarantees of $6+\delta$ and about $12.33$. Separately, the configuration-LP analysis proves that allocations within a factor about $3.808$ of optimum exist, but the proof does not yield a polynomial-time algorithm with that ratio. The open problem is to close this constructive-versus-existential gap, or to prove that polynomial-time algorithms face a larger barrier.

where_posed.text: The best polynomial-time ratio 4 + delta is from Cheng & Mao, 'Restricted Max-Min Allocation: Integrality Gap and Approximation Algorithm,' Algorithmica (ICALP 2019); existential ~3.808 via the configuration LP. Closing the constructive-existential gap is open.

problems.title: Monotonicity in T of the tight k-unit IID prophet ratio
problems.short_statement: For the i.i.d. prophet inequality accepting up to $k$ of $T$ values, an LP captures the tight online-to-offline ratio. Numerically the ratio decreases in $T$; the conjecture is to prove this monotonicity for general $k$, which would fully characterize the tight $k$-unit IID prophet guarantee.
problems.open_problem: Show that for each fixed $k$, $\mathrm{val}(k,T)$ is decreasing in $T$ (more arrivals make the gambler's task relatively harder). For $k=1$ this monotonicity was established by Correa et al.
problems.full_statement: Consider the IID prophet inequality with $T$ values and capacity $k$: T values arrive i.i.d. from a distribution $\mathbf F$, and the gambler may accept up to $k$ of them online, comparing against the offline optimum. Let $\mathrm{val}(k,T)=\inf_{\mathbf F}\mathrm{ONL}/\mathrm{OFF}$ be the tight competitive ratio (online versus offline) for given $k$ and $T$, captured by a known LP (the LP of the notes without the normalization constraint). \textbf{Open:} Show that for each fixed $k$, $\mathrm{val}(k,T)$ is decreasing in $T$ (more arrivals make the gambler's task relatively harder). For $k=1$ this monotonicity was established by Correa et al. (2017); the general $k$ case is open. Proving it would fully characterize the tight $k$-unit IID prophet guarantee.
problems.known_results: Established for $k = 1$ (Correa-Foncea-Hoeksma-Oosterwijk-Vredeveld 2017; the tight single-item IID constant is ~0.745, problem known since Hill-Kertz 1982). Proving monotonicity in T for general k is open and would fully characterize the tight k-unit IID prophet inequality.

problems.short_statement: For the $k$-unit prophet inequality under adversarial order, the online-to-fluid ratio is understood, but the online-to-offline ratio appears different and is not understood for $k>1$. The open problem asks for any nontrivial bound, even restricting to weighted-Bernoulli distributions.
problems.open_problem: Determine (or bound) $\inf_{T,\mathbf F}\mathrm{ONL}/\mathrm{OFF}$ for $k>1$, even restricting each value distribution to be weighted Bernoulli (a two-point distribution with one point at 0).
problems.full_statement: Consider the $k$-unit prophet inequality under adversarial arrival order: values from distributions $\mathbf F$ arrive in an adversarial order over $T$ steps, and an online algorithm may accept up to $k$. Let $\mathrm{ONL}$ be the online value, $\mathrm{FLU}$ the fluid/LP benchmark, and $\mathrm{OFF}$ the true offline optimum. Under adversarial order, $\inf_{T,\mathbf F}\mathrm{ONL}/\mathrm{FLU}$ is known for $k$-unit prophet inequalities, but $\inf_{T,\mathbf F}\mathrm{ONL}/\mathrm{OFF}$ is not, and the two appear to differ (Jiang-Ma-Zhang 2022). \textbf{Open:} Determine (or bound) $\inf_{T,\mathbf F}\mathrm{ONL}/\mathrm{OFF}$ for $k>1$, even restricting each value distribution to be weighted Bernoulli (a two-point distribution with one point at 0).
problems.known_results: The online-to-fluid ratio inf ONL/FLU is tight/understood for k-unit prophet; the online-to-offline ratio inf ONL/OFF is open for $k > 1$ and appears different (Jiang-Ma-Zhang 2022, Prop. 1 of arXiv version). Even the weighted-Bernoulli special case is open.

problems.title: Overcoming the correlation gap for k-unit prophet under random order (k>1)
problems.short_statement: For $k$-unit prophet inequalities, the correlation-gap constant $c_k$ can be beaten when the algorithm freely chooses the arrival order. Under random order the best known bound is stuck exactly at $c_k$ for $k>1$ (only $k=1$ is beatable). The open problem is to extend the free-order techniques to random order for $k>1$.
problems.open_problem: Can the order-selection techniques be extended to overcome the correlation gap under random order for $k>1$? Any improvement would establish a separation for $\inf \mathrm{ONL}/\mathrm{OFF}$ previously unknown.
problems.full_statement: For $k$-unit prophet inequalities, let $c_k^{\mathrm{CG}} = 1 - e^{-k}k^k/k!$ be the correlation-gap constant, and let $\mathrm{ONL}/\mathrm{OFF}$ denote the online-to-offline ratio. This constant can be overcome (beaten) when the arrival order can be freely chosen by the algorithm. Under the slightly harder random arrival order, the best-known $\inf_{T,\mathbf F}\mathrm{ONL}/\mathrm{OFF}$ for $k$-unit prophet is stuck at $c_k^{\mathrm{CG}}$ for $k>1$ (Arnosti-Ma 2022), while it is beaten for $k=1$ (Correa et al. 2021). \textbf{Open:} Can the order-selection techniques be extended to overcome the correlation gap under random order for $k>1$? Any improvement would establish a separation for $\inf \mathrm{ONL}/\mathrm{OFF}$ previously unknown.
problems.known_results: For $k=1$, Correa and coauthors show that the correlation-gap constant can be beaten. For $k>1$ under random arrival order, the best known online-to-offline guarantees remain stuck at the correlation-gap value $c_k^{\mathrm{CG}}$, as in the Arnosti-Ma analysis of static thresholds. The open problem is to beat $c_k^{\mathrm{CG}}$ for any fixed $k>1$ under random order; even an arbitrarily small improvement would establish a new separation between random order and the correlation-gap barrier.

problems.title: The stockout-probability static-threshold conjecture
problems.short_statement: For the $k$-unit IID prophet problem, Arnosti and Ma conjecture (and verify numerically) that setting a single static threshold so the stockout probability $\Pr[D \ge k]$ equals $\Pr[\mathrm{Pois}(k) \ge k]$ achieves the tight correlation-gap guarantee. The open problem is to prove this or find a counterexample.
problems.open_problem: Prove this (e.g. via Bernoulli optimization) or find a counterexample.
problems.full_statement: In the $k$-unit IID setting (T values arrive i.i.d.; accept up to $k$), consider static-threshold policies indexed by the threshold's induced demand distribution $D$ (the number of values above the threshold). \textbf{Conjecture (Arnosti-Ma):} setting the threshold so that $\Pr[D \ge k] = \Pr[\mathrm{Pois}(k) \ge k]$ achieves the tight guarantee $c_k^{\mathrm{CG}} = 1 - e^{-k}k^k/k!$ under random order. \textbf{Open:} Prove this (e.g. via Bernoulli optimization) or find a counterexample.
problems.known_results: Arnosti and Ma formulate this threshold rule and verify it numerically for the relevant Bernoulli-optimization problem. Their evidence supports the conjecture that the stockout-probability threshold achieves the tight correlation-gap guarantee $c_k^{\mathrm{CG}}$ for every $k$. What is missing is a proof that this particular threshold is always worst-case optimal, or a counterexample showing that the numerical pattern eventually breaks.

problems.title: Guarantee 1/2 with a static threshold without randomization or cardinal values
problems.short_statement: In the single-item prophet inequality, the $1/2$ guarantee is achievable by a deterministic static threshold, and separately knowing only ordinal (median) information. Open: can both restrictions hold at once - a static threshold guaranteeing $1/2$ using neither randomization nor cardinal values?
problems.open_problem: Is there a static-threshold policy guaranteeing $\mathbb{E}[\mathrm{ALG}] \ge \tfrac12\,\mathbb{E}[\max_i X_i]$ that uses neither randomization nor knowledge of cardinal values? Formally, the algorithm is told only $\boldsymbol\Lambda \in \Delta([n])^T$ with $r_1 \ge \cdots \ge r_n \ge 0$, must commit to a threshold defined by an index $J \in [n]$ (and possibly a deterministic tie-break), and then $r_1,\dots,r_n$ are set adversarially.
problems.full_statement: In the classic single-item (single-choice, $k=1$) prophet inequality, the $1/2$ guarantee can be achieved by a deterministic static threshold, and separately can be achieved knowing only ordinal (median) information rather than exact cardinal values. \textbf{Open:} Is there a static-threshold policy guaranteeing $\mathbb{E}[\mathrm{ALG}] \ge \tfrac12\,\mathbb{E}[\max_i X_i]$ that uses neither randomization nor knowledge of cardinal values? Formally, the algorithm is told only $\boldsymbol\Lambda \in \Delta([n])^T$ with $r_1 \ge \cdots \ge r_n \ge 0$, must commit to a threshold defined by an index $J \in [n]$ (and possibly a deterministic tie-break), and then $r_1,\dots,r_n$ are set adversarially.
problems.known_results: $1/2$ is achievable by a deterministic static threshold (knowing cardinal values) and, separately, knowing only the median (Samuel-Cahn 1984). Whether $1/2$ is achievable simultaneously without randomization and without cardinal values is open.

problems.title: Is unknown uniformly random order ever worse than worst-case known order?
problems.short_statement: Intuitively, an algorithm facing values in unknown uniformly random order should do at least as well as facing an adversary's worst order that it is told in advance. The open question: can this ever fail for some product distribution? Resolving that it never does would derive random-order prophet inequalities from fixed-order analysis.
problems.open_problem: does there exist a product distribution $\mathbf F \in \Delta(\mathbb{R}_{\ge 0})^T$ for which $\sup_{\pi \in \Pi^T} \mathbb{E}_\sigma \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf V \sim \mathbf F}\,\mathrm{ALG}^\pi(\sigma(\mathbf V)) < \min_\sigma \sup_{\pi \in \Pi^T} \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf V \sim \mathbf F}\,\mathrm{ALG}^\pi(\sigma(\mathbf V))$? (Equation (C.1) of the notes.) Equivalently: can unknown random order be strictly worse than the worst-case known order?
problems.full_statement: When values arrive in an unknown uniformly random order, intuitively the algorithm should do at least as well as if an adversary picked the worst order but told the algorithm what it was. For the single-choice ($k=1$) accept/reject problem, \textbf{Open:} does there exist a product distribution $\mathbf F \in \Delta(\mathbb{R}_{\ge 0})^T$ for which $\sup_{\pi \in \Pi^T} \mathbb{E}_\sigma \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf V \sim \mathbf F}\,\mathrm{ALG}^\pi(\sigma(\mathbf V)) < \min_\sigma \sup_{\pi \in \Pi^T} \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf V \sim \mathbf F}\,\mathrm{ALG}^\pi(\sigma(\mathbf V))$? (Equation (C.1) of the notes.) Equivalently: can unknown random order be strictly worse than the worst-case known order? Resolving that it never happens would let one derive random-order prophet inequalities from the simpler fixed-order analysis.
problems.known_results: No counterexample is known, and the prevailing intuition is that unknown uniformly random order should not be worse than the adversary's worst order when that order is revealed in advance. Proving this would be useful because fixed-order prophet-inequality analyses could then transfer directly to random-order models. The difficulty is that the algorithm in the known-order benchmark can tailor its stopping rule to the revealed permutation, while the random-order algorithm must average over all permutations without seeing which one will occur.

problems.title: Are ex-ante prophet inequalities and OCRS equivalent under oblivious adversarial order?
problems.short_statement: Online contention resolution schemes (OCRS) and ex-ante prophet inequalities give equivalent guarantees under random and fixed order. The open question is whether the same equivalence holds under oblivious adversarial order: prove it for a general feasibility family and polytope relaxation, or give a counterexample.
problems.open_problem: For a feasibility family $\mathcal F$, a standard polytope relaxation $\mathcal P$, and $\mathbf x \in \mathcal P$, are the best ex-ante prophet inequality guarantee and the best OCRS selectability equal under oblivious adversarial arrival order (as they are under random and fixed order)? Prove the equivalence for a general feasibility family and polytope relaxation, or exhibit a counterexample showing the two notions diverge.
problems.full_statement: Online contention resolution schemes (OCRS) and ex-ante prophet inequalities are known to give equivalent guarantees under random order and under fixed order. \textbf{Open:} For a feasibility family $\mathcal F$, a standard polytope relaxation $\mathcal P$, and $\mathbf x \in \mathcal P$, are the best ex-ante prophet inequality guarantee and the best OCRS selectability equal under oblivious adversarial arrival order (as they are under random and fixed order)? Prove the equivalence for a general feasibility family and polytope relaxation, or exhibit a counterexample showing the two notions diverge.
problems.known_results: Lee and Singla established the equivalence between ex-ante prophet inequalities and online contention resolution schemes under random order and under fixed order. The oblivious-adversarial-order model sits between these regimes: the order is not adaptive to the realizations, but it is not random either. It is open whether the same reduction still goes through in this setting or whether the two notions separate.

problems.title: A 1/2-selectable OCRS for rank-1 matroids against the almighty adversary
problems.short_statement: A simple $1/2$-selectable OCRS is known for the rank-1 matroid against the standard online adversary. Open: whether a $1/2$-selectable OCRS exists against the much stronger almighty adversary (which sees all realizations); the best known is only $1/4$, and even beating $1/4$ is open.
problems.open_problem: For the rank-1 matroid, does there exist a $\tfrac12$-selectable OCRS against the almighty adversary (which knows all realized values in advance)? An ex-ante prophet inequality guarantees $\tfrac12$ against the almighty adversary on rank-1 matroids, but the best-known OCRS against the almighty adversary is only $\tfrac14$ (Feldman et al.
problems.full_statement: A simple $\tfrac12$-selectable online contention resolution scheme is known for the rank-1 matroid (single-choice) against the standard online adversary. \textbf{Open:} For the rank-1 matroid, does there exist a $\tfrac12$-selectable OCRS against the almighty adversary (which knows all realized values in advance)? An ex-ante prophet inequality guarantees $\tfrac12$ against the almighty adversary on rank-1 matroids, but the best-known OCRS against the almighty adversary is only $\tfrac14$ (Feldman et al. 2021). Either show a 1/2-selectable OCRS exists, find a modified one, or prove impossibility; even beating 1/4 is open.
problems.known_results: For rank-1 matroids, Feldman, Svensson, and Zenklusen give a $1/4$-selectable OCRS against the almighty adversary. In contrast, the corresponding ex-ante prophet-inequality guarantee is $1/2$, so the OCRS and prophet views do not currently match quantitatively in this adversarial model. It is open whether one can close the gap all the way to $1/2$, or even improve the OCRS constant beyond $1/4$.

problems.title: An explicit k-unit OCRS achieving the correlation-gap guarantee under random order
problems.short_statement: For $k$-unit constraints under random order, a $c_k$-selectable OCRS is known to exist but only via a non-explicit existence proof (through ex-ante prophet inequalities). The open problem is to construct an explicit $c_k$-selectable OCRS for $k>1$; an explicit scheme is known only for $k=1$.
problems.open_problem: Under random order with a $k$-unit constraint, construct an explicit $c_k^{\mathrm{CG}}$-selectable OCRS, where $c_k^{\mathrm{CG}} = 1 - e^{-k}k^k/k!$. Such an OCRS is known to exist only via an existential argument; an explicit description is known only for $k=1$ (Lee-Singla 2018, Apx.
problems.full_statement: For $k$-unit constraints under random arrival order, a $c_k$-selectable OCRS is known to exist, but only via a non-explicit existence proof (going through ex-ante prophet inequalities). \textbf{Open:} Under random order with a $k$-unit constraint, construct an explicit $c_k^{\mathrm{CG}}$-selectable OCRS, where $c_k^{\mathrm{CG}} = 1 - e^{-k}k^k/k!$. Such an OCRS is known to exist only via an existential argument; an explicit description is known only for $k=1$ (Lee-Singla 2018, Apx. A.2 of arXiv version).
problems.known_results: A $c_k^{\mathrm{CG}}$-selectable OCRS is known to exist under random order because one can pass through the ex-ante prophet-inequality equivalence. That argument is not an explicit online rule, however. Lee and Singla give an explicit construction for the single-unit case $k=1$, but no comparable construction is known for $k>1$. The open problem is to turn the existential guarantee into a usable OCRS that achieves the same correlation-gap constant.

problems.title: Tight guarantee for online knapsack (unit-density / IID): is it (1-e^{-2})/2?
problems.short_statement: In online knapsack, items must be irrevocably accepted or rejected. For the unit-density (reward = size) and IID special cases, the best known guarantee relative to the fluid benchmark is $\approx 0.355$ and no policy beats $\approx 0.432$. The conjecture is that the tight guarantee equals $(1 - e^{-2})/2 \approx 0.432$.
problems.open_problem: Determine the tight competitive ratio for unit-density online stochastic knapsack; in particular, prove or refute the conjectured value $(1-e^{-2})/2$.
problems.full_statement: In the online knapsack problem, items with sizes and rewards arrive and must be irrevocably accepted (consuming capacity) or rejected. For online knapsack with the fluid relaxation as benchmark, in the unit-density case ($r_j = s_j$, reward equals size) or the IID case, the best known guarantee is $0.355$ (Jiang-Ma-Zhang 2022) improving $0.321$ (Stein et al. 2020), with an upper bound of $(1-e^{-2})/2 \approx 0.432$. \textbf{Conjecture:} the tight guarantee equals $(1-e^{-2})/2$. Determine it.
problems.known_results: For unit-density online knapsack, Stein, Truong, and Wang proved a $0.321$ guarantee, later improved to $0.355$ by Jiang, Ma, and Zhang. The known upper bound is $(1-e^{-2})/2\approx 0.432$, and this value is the natural conjectured tight constant. For general rewards, no guarantee better than $1/(3+e^{-2})\approx 0.319$ is known even under random order or IID assumptions. The open problem is to close the gap for unit-density knapsack, ideally proving or refuting the conjectured $(1-e^{-2})/2$ constant.

problems.title: An L-sparse network revenue management guarantee interpolating in starting capacity
problems.short_statement: In $L$-sparse network revenue management, each product consumes one unit of up to $L$ resources. A simple scheme guarantees $1/(1+L)$ of the fluid benchmark, and a recent bound gives asymptotic optimality as the minimum starting capacity $k$ grows. The open problem is a single guarantee interpolating between the two regimes.
problems.open_problem: Find a guarantee that equals $1/(1+L)$ at $k=1$ and approaches $1$ as $k \to \infty$ (i.e. smoothly interpolating between the two regimes).
problems.full_statement: In $L$-sparse network revenue management, each product consumes one unit of up to $L$ different resources. For $L$-sparse NRM, $\mathbb{E}[\mathrm{ALG}] \ge \mathrm{FLU}/(1+L)$ holds under adversarial order, and $\mathbb{E}[\mathrm{ALG}] \ge (1 - L/\sqrt{k+3})\,\mathrm{FLU}$ (Amil-Makhdoumi-Wei 2022) gives asymptotic optimality (approaching 1) as $k = \min_i k_i \to \infty$ for fixed $L$, but is vacuous when $k=1, L>1$. \textbf{Open:} Find a guarantee that equals $1/(1+L)$ at $k=1$ and approaches $1$ as $k \to \infty$ (i.e. smoothly interpolating between the two regimes).
problems.known_results: Under adversarial order, a $1/(1+L)$ guarantee is known, and a folklore upper bound of $2/(1+L)$ shows that some linear dependence on $L$ is unavoidable when capacities are small. In the large-capacity regime, Amil, Makhdoumi, and Wei prove asymptotic optimality as the minimum starting capacity $k$ grows. The open problem is to find a single guarantee that recovers the small-capacity $1/(1+L)$ behavior and also approaches $1$ as $k$ grows.

problems.title: Any nonzero guarantee for the most general L-sparse knapsack
problems.short_statement: The most general structure here is $L$-sparse knapsack: each product consumes a positive amount of up to $L$ resources. Nothing is known about online guarantees in this setting. The open problem asks for an adversarial-order guarantee that is $\approx 0.319$ when $L=1$ and remains nonzero for all $L$.
problems.open_problem: is it possible to derive an adversarial-order guarantee that is $1/(3+e^{-2}) \approx 0.319$ when $L=1$ and nonzero for all $L$? Currently nothing is known in this most general setting.
problems.full_statement: The most general constraint structure in this framework is $L$-sparse knapsack: each product consumes a positive amount of up to $L$ different resources. For $L$-sparse knapsack (each product consumes a positive amount $s_{ij}\in[0,1]$ of up to $L$ resources, i.e. $|\{i: s_{ij}>0\}| \le L$), with the fluid relaxation as benchmark, \textbf{Open:} is it possible to derive an adversarial-order guarantee that is $1/(3+e^{-2}) \approx 0.319$ when $L=1$ and nonzero for all $L$? Currently nothing is known in this most general setting.
problems.known_results: For $L=1$, which is the single-resource online stochastic knapsack setting, the best-fit policy gives the tight adversarial-order guarantee $1/(3+e^{-2})\approx 0.319$ relative to the fluid benchmark. For general $L$-sparse knapsack, where each product can consume positive amounts of several resources, no nonzero guarantee is currently known in this level of generality. The open problem is to obtain any meaningful adversarial-order guarantee for all $L$, ideally one that reduces to the tight $L=1$ constant.

problems.title: A strongly polynomial algorithm for linear programming (Smale's 9th problem)
problems.short_statement: Is there an algorithm that solves every linear program using a number of arithmetic operations bounded by a polynomial in the number of variables and constraints alone — independent of the bit-length of the data?
problems.open_problem: Question (Smale's 9th problem): does a strongly polynomial algorithm for general linear programming exist?
problems.full_statement: Consider a linear program $\max\{c^\top x : Ax \le b\}$ with rational data $A \in \mathbb{Q}^{m\times n}$, $b\in\mathbb{Q}^m$, $c\in\mathbb{Q}^n$. An algorithm is \emph{strongly polynomial} if (i) it uses a number of elementary arithmetic operations $(+,-,\times,\div,\le)$ bounded by a polynomial in $m$ and $n$ only, and (ii) the numbers occurring in the computation have bit-size bounded by a polynomial in the input size. Khachiyan's ellipsoid method (1979) and Karmarkar's interior-point method (1984) are \emph{weakly} polynomial: their operation count depends on the bit-length $L$ of the data. Question (Smale's 9th problem): does a strongly polynomial algorithm for general linear programming exist?
problems.known_results: Strongly polynomial algorithms are known for important special cases: combinatorial LPs where entries of $A$ are bounded (Tardos 1986, depending only on $A$), LPs with at most two nonzeros per row or column, and fixed dimension (Megiddo). Recent layered/interior-point advances (e.g., Dadush–Huiberts–Natura–Végh) give bounds depending on the constraint-matrix condition number. The general case is open.

where_posed.text: Posed as Problem 9 in S. Smale, 'Mathematical problems for the next century' (1998); long studied since Tardos (1986).

source.title: Mathematical Problems for the Next Century

problems.title: A polynomial pivot rule for the simplex method
problems.short_statement: Is there a pivot rule under which the simplex method always solves a linear program in a number of pivot steps bounded by a polynomial in the number of variables and constraints?
problems.open_problem: does there exist a (deterministic or randomized) pivot rule for which the number of pivot steps is bounded by a polynomial in $m$ and $n$ for every linear program?
problems.full_statement: The simplex method solves $\max\{c^\top x : Ax\le b,\ x\ge 0\}$ by walking along edges of the feasible polyhedron from vertex to adjacent improving vertex; a \emph{pivot rule} chooses, at each vertex, which improving edge to follow. Klee and Minty (1972) showed Dantzig's most-negative-reduced-cost rule can take $2^n-1$ steps on a deformed cube, and analogous exponential lower bounds are known for essentially every classical deterministic rule (Bland, steepest edge, greatest improvement, etc.). Question: does there exist a (deterministic or randomized) pivot rule for which the number of pivot steps is bounded by a polynomial in $m$ and $n$ for every linear program?
problems.known_results: Best known are subexponential randomized rules: Random-Facet achieves $\exp(O(\sqrt{n\log n}))$ pivots (Kalai 1992; Matoušek–Sharir–Welzl), later shown essentially tight for such rules (Friedmann–Hansen–Zwick). No polynomial pivot rule is known. The question is intimately tied to the polynomial Hirsch conjecture (a polynomial diameter bound is necessary for any polynomial pivot rule).

where_posed.text: A central open problem of linear optimization since Klee–Minty (1972); see Kalai (1992) and Todd's surveys.

source.title: How Good is the Simplex Algorithm?

problems.title: The Polynomial Hirsch Conjecture
problems.short_statement: Is the combinatorial diameter of the graph of a $d$-dimensional polytope with $n$ facets bounded by a polynomial in $n$ and $d$?
problems.open_problem: The \emph{polynomial Hirsch conjecture} asks: is there a polynomial $p$ such that the diameter is at most $p(n,d)$ for every polytope?
problems.full_statement: Let $P\subseteq\mathbb{R}^d$ be a convex polytope defined by $n$ facet inequalities. Its \emph{graph} has the vertices of $P$ as nodes, with an edge between two vertices if they share an edge (1-dimensional face) of $P$. The \emph{combinatorial diameter} of $P$ is the maximum, over all pairs of vertices, of the graph distance between them. The Hirsch conjecture (1957) asserted this diameter is at most $n-d$; Santos (2012) constructed a counterexample. The \emph{polynomial Hirsch conjecture} asks: is there a polynomial $p$ such that the diameter is at most $p(n,d)$ for every polytope? A positive answer is a prerequisite for any polynomial-time pivot rule for the simplex method.
problems.known_results: Upper bounds are only quasi-polynomial: the Kalai–Kleitman bound $n^{\log_2 d + O(1)}$ (1992), refined by Todd to $(n-d)^{\log_2 d}$ and by Sukegawa. Linear-in-$n$ bounds are known for special classes. Lower bounds are linear. Whether any polynomial upper bound holds is open.

where_posed.text: The Hirsch conjecture (Dantzig 1957) was disproved by Santos (2012); the polynomial version is the surviving central question (popularized via Polymath3, Kalai).

source.title: A counterexample to the Hirsch conjecture

problems.title: A strongly polynomial algorithm for Markov decision processes
problems.short_statement: Is there a strongly polynomial algorithm for discounted Markov decision processes when the discount factor is part of the input (equivalently, is policy iteration polynomial for input discount)?
problems.open_problem: when $\gamma$ is part of the input, is policy iteration polynomial — and more broadly, does any strongly polynomial algorithm exist for MDPs (a natural special case on the road to strongly polynomial LP)?
problems.full_statement: A discounted MDP has a finite state set $S$, finite action sets $A_s$, transition probabilities $p(s'\,|\,s,a)$, rewards $r(s,a)$, and discount factor $\gamma\in(0,1)$; the goal is a stationary policy maximizing expected discounted reward $\mathbb{E}[\sum_{t\ge0}\gamma^t r(s_t,a_t)]$. This is a linear program with $|S|$ variables. Ye (2011) proved that Howard's policy-iteration method and the simplex method (Dantzig rule) are strongly polynomial when $\gamma$ is a fixed constant, with complexity bounded by a polynomial in $|S|$ and $\sum_s|A_s|$ but blowing up as $\gamma\to1$. Question: when $\gamma$ is part of the input, is policy iteration polynomial — and more broadly, does any strongly polynomial algorithm exist for MDPs (a natural special case on the road to strongly polynomial LP)?
problems.known_results: Ye (2011) gives strong polynomiality for fixed $\gamma$ (bound $\sim \frac{|S|^2\,|A|}{1-\gamma}\log\frac{|S|}{1-\gamma}$). Post–Ye showed the simplex method is strongly polynomial for deterministic MDPs regardless of $\gamma$. For general MDPs with input $\gamma$ (or average-reward), strong polynomiality is open; Ye explicitly notes it remains open whether policy iteration is polynomial in this regime, or whether a strongly polynomial algorithm exists for MDPs or LP in general.

where_posed.text: Stated as an open question in Y. Ye, 'The Simplex and Policy-Iteration Methods Are Strongly Polynomial for the MDP with a Fixed Discount Rate' (Math. of OR 2011).

source.title: The Simplex and Policy-Iteration Methods Are Strongly Polynomial for the Markov Decision Problem with a Fixed Discount Rate

problems.title: The exact value of the Beardwood–Halton–Hammersley TSP constant
problems.short_statement: For $n$ points drawn uniformly at random in the unit square, the shortest tour length divided by $\sqrt{n}$ converges to a universal constant $\beta$. What is the exact value of $\beta$?
problems.open_problem: Determine the exact value of $\beta$ (or give matching rigorous upper and lower bounds).
problems.full_statement: Let $X_1,\dots,X_n$ be independent and uniformly distributed in the unit square $[0,1]^2$, and let $L_n$ denote the length of the shortest closed tour (the optimal Euclidean Traveling Salesman tour) visiting all of them. Beardwood, Halton, and Hammersley (1959) proved that there is a constant $\beta>0$ with $L_n/\sqrt{n}\to\beta$ almost surely as $n\to\infty$. Determine the exact value of $\beta$ (or give matching rigorous upper and lower bounds).
problems.known_results: Monte-Carlo estimates give $\beta\approx 0.7124$. The best rigorous bounds are roughly $0.6277 \le \beta \le 0.9038$ (lower bound via Gaudio–Jaillet 2019 / Steele-type arguments; upper bound Carlsson–Yu 2023), with arguments suggesting the current upper-bound technique cannot go below $\approx 0.88$. The exact value is unknown.

where_posed.text: Originates in Beardwood–Halton–Hammersley (1959); a long-standing open problem highlighted in Steele, 'Probability Theory and Combinatorial Optimization' (1997).

source.authors: Jillian Beardwood, J. H. Halton, J. M. Hammersley

problems.title: Complexity and approximability of two-stage stochastic programming
problems.short_statement: What is the exact computational complexity of evaluating/optimizing a two-stage stochastic linear program, and does it admit an efficient (e.g. fully polynomial randomized) approximation scheme?
problems.open_problem: pin down the exact complexity of approximating the optimal value — in particular, does the problem admit a fully polynomial randomized approximation scheme (FPRAS), or is approximation itself #P-hard? Relatedly, characterize the tight sample size needed by Sample Average Approximation (SAA) to guarantee an $\epsilon$-optimal solution with high probability.
problems.full_statement: A two-stage stochastic linear program is $\min_{x\in X}\; c^\top x + \mathbb{E}_{\xi}\,[\,Q(x,\xi)\,]$, where the recourse cost is $Q(x,\xi)=\min\{q^\top y : Wy \ge h(\xi)-T(\xi)x,\ y\ge 0\}$ and $\xi$ is a random vector with a known distribution (e.g. independent components). The difficulty is evaluating the expectation $\mathbb{E}_\xi[Q(x,\xi)]$, a high-dimensional integral. Question: pin down the exact complexity of approximating the optimal value — in particular, does the problem admit a fully polynomial randomized approximation scheme (FPRAS), or is approximation itself #P-hard? Relatedly, characterize the tight sample size needed by Sample Average Approximation (SAA) to guarantee an $\epsilon$-optimal solution with high probability.
problems.known_results: Dyer–Stougie (2006) argued two-stage stochastic programming is #P-hard and multistage is PSPACE-hard under explicit independent distributions (a subsequent note by Hanasusanto–Kuhn–Wiesemann (2016) corrected/clarified parts of the two-stage argument). SAA enjoys exponential convergence of the probability of a bad solution in the sample size, with sample complexity polynomial in dimension and $1/\epsilon^2$ for fixed classes (Kleywegt–Shapiro–Homem-de-Mello; Shapiro–Nemirovski). The precise complexity/approximability and tight sample bounds for general two-stage problems remain unsettled.

where_posed.text: Complexity questions posed in Dyer & Stougie, 'Computational complexity of stochastic programming problems' (Math. Programming 2006); see also the comment of Hanasusanto–Kuhn–Wiesemann (2016).

source.title: Computational complexity of stochastic programming problems
