problems.title: Exact optimality of the generalized cμ rule for convex delay costs
problems.short_statement: For scheduling a multiclass queue with convex delay costs, the generalized $c\mu$ rule is optimal in heavy traffic; is it (or some clean index policy) exactly optimal outside the heavy-traffic limit?
problems.open_problem: is the generalized $c\mu$ rule (or another explicit index policy) exactly optimal for the original (non-asymptotic) stochastic control problem, and if not, characterize the exact optimal policy?
problems.full_statement: Consider a multiclass queue: customer classes $i=1,\dots,K$ with queue lengths $Q_i(t)$, service rates $\mu_i$ (or rates $\mu_{ij}$ on flexible servers $j$), and class-$i$ delay cost given by a nondecreasing convex function $C_i(\cdot)$. The objective is to choose a (possibly preemptive) scheduling policy minimizing the expected cumulative cost $\mathbb{E}\int_0^T \sum_i C_i(Q_i(t))\,dt$ (or its long-run average). The generalized $c\mu$ rule serves, whenever a server is free, a class in $\arg\max_i C_i'(Q_i(t))\,\mu_i$. Van Mieghem (1995) proved this rule is asymptotically optimal in heavy traffic. Question: is the generalized $c\mu$ rule (or another explicit index policy) exactly optimal for the original (non-asymptotic) stochastic control problem, and if not, characterize the exact optimal policy?
problems.known_results: Heavy-traffic (diffusion-scale) asymptotic optimality is established for single flexible server (Van Mieghem 1995) and multi-server (Mandelbaum–Stolyar 2004). For linear costs $C_i(x)=c_i x$ the rule reduces to the classical $c\mu$ rule, which is exactly optimal (Klimov / Cox–Smith). For strictly convex costs, exact optimality is known only in special cases; the general exact-optimal policy is open.

where_posed.text: The exact- vs asymptotic-optimality gap is discussed in Van Mieghem, 'Dynamic Scheduling with Convex Delay Costs: The Generalized c-mu Rule' (Annals of Applied Probability 1995).

source.title: Dynamic Scheduling with Convex Delay Costs: The Generalized c-mu Rule

problems.title: Optimality of smallest-variance-first sequencing in appointment scheduling
problems.short_statement: When sequencing customers with stochastic service times at a single server, is it optimal to order them by increasing variance (smallest-variance-first)?
problems.open_problem: is SVF sequencing optimal for general $n$ (for exponential service times, and more generally under a suitable variability/dispersion ordering)?
problems.full_statement: A single server must serve $n$ customers (e.g. patients, surgeries) whose service times $S_1,\dots,S_n$ are independent with known distributions. The customers are placed in a sequence and given appointment times; the cost is the expected weighted sum of customer waiting times and server idle time (and possibly overtime). For exponentially distributed service times, Wang (1999) proved that sequencing customers in increasing order of variance — equivalently increasing mean for exponentials — minimizes expected total cost for $n=2$, and conjectured optimality of this 'smallest-variance-first' (SVF) rule for all $n$. Question: is SVF sequencing optimal for general $n$ (for exponential service times, and more generally under a suitable variability/dispersion ordering)?
problems.known_results: Weiss (1990) introduced a dispersion measure, proved an ordering optimal for $n=2$, and disproved his measure's optimality at $n=3$. Wang (1999) established SVF optimality for $n=2$ with exponential service times and conjectured it for all $n$. Partial and asymptotic results and counterexamples in variants exist; the general-$n$ optimality of SVF remains open.

where_posed.text: Conjectured in P. P. Wang, 'Sequencing and scheduling N customers for a stochastic server' (EJOR 1999); foundations in Weiss (1990).

problems.title: Best approximation ratio for constrained assortment under the Markov-chain choice model
problems.short_statement: What is the best possible polynomial-time approximation ratio for cardinality- or capacity-constrained assortment optimization under the Markov-chain choice model?
problems.open_problem: determine the best achievable polynomial-time approximation ratio for the constrained problem.
problems.full_statement: In the Markov-chain choice model (Blanchet–Gallego–Goyal 2016), there are $n$ substitutable products with prices $p_i$. An arriving customer initially considers product $i$ with probability $\lambda_i$ (with $\sum_i \lambda_i + \lambda_0 = 1$, where $0$ is the no-purchase option); if the considered product is not offered, the customer transitions to product $j$ with probability $\rho_{ij}$, repeating until reaching an offered product (a purchase) or the no-purchase state. Given a feasibility constraint on the offered set $S$ — a cardinality bound $|S|\le k$, or knapsack/capacity weights $\sum_{i\in S} w_i \le W$ — choose $S$ to maximize expected revenue. The unconstrained problem is polynomial-time solvable; the constrained problem is APX-hard. Question: determine the best achievable polynomial-time approximation ratio for the constrained problem.
problems.known_results: Désir–Goyal–Segev–Ye (2020) prove the capacity-constrained problem is APX-hard (so no PTAS unless P=NP) and give constant-factor approximation algorithms for the cardinality- and capacity-constrained versions. The exact best-possible ratio — closing the gap between the achievable constant and the APX-hardness threshold — is open.

where_posed.text: Open gap left by Désir, Goyal, Segev, Ye, 'Constrained Assortment Optimization Under the Markov Chain-based Choice Model' (Management Science 2020).

problems.title: A simple optimal policy for perishable inventory with fixed lifetime ≥ 3
problems.short_statement: For periodic-review inventory of a product that expires after a fixed number of periods ($\ge 3$), is any simple ordering policy exactly or asymptotically optimal?
problems.open_problem: does there exist a simple, tractable policy (e.g. an order-up-to rule in total inventory, or a critical-number policy) that is exactly optimal for $m\ge 3$, or that is provably asymptotically optimal with explicit performance guarantees?
problems.full_statement: Consider a single item under periodic review whose units perish exactly $m$ periods after arrival (fixed shelf life), with i.i.d. demand, FIFO issuance, and linear holding, outdating (expiry), and shortage (or lost-sales) costs. The state needed for the dynamic program is the age profile of on-hand stock — the vector $(x_1,\dots,x_{m-1})$ of quantities with $1,\dots,m-1$ periods of remaining life — so the optimal order quantity is a function of this whole vector. For $m\ge 3$ the optimal policy is not of base-stock/order-up-to form and is computationally intractable (curse of dimensionality). Question: does there exist a simple, tractable policy (e.g. an order-up-to rule in total inventory, or a critical-number policy) that is exactly optimal for $m\ge 3$, or that is provably asymptotically optimal with explicit performance guarantees?
problems.known_results: The two-period case $m=2$ is well understood (Nahmias–Pierskalla 1973). For $m\ge 3$ no simple optimal policy is known; Nahmias (1976) gives an effective approximate critical-number heuristic. Recent work proves asymptotic optimality of base-stock policies (as the outdating/penalty cost structure or lifetime grows) and of constant-order policies in certain regimes, but a simple policy that is exactly optimal, or asymptotically optimal with tight finite-horizon guarantees in general, remains open.

where_posed.text: Open problems highlighted in S. Nahmias, 'Perishable Inventory Theory: A Review' (Operations Research 1982).

problems.title: Optimal policy in dynamic matching markets with stochastic arrivals and departures
problems.short_statement: In a market where agents arrive and depart over time and can only be matched to compatible partners present at the same time, what is the optimal matching policy — and exactly how valuable is waiting to 'thicken' the market?
problems.open_problem: characterize the optimal dynamic matching policy and the exact achievable loss in the general model — beyond the restricted class and beyond the specific sparsity regime.
problems.full_statement: Agents arrive to a market as a Poisson process of rate $m$; each ordered/unordered pair of present agents is compatible independently with a small probability (yielding a sparse random compatibility graph with average degree $d$); each agent, if not yet matched, departs after an independent $\mathrm{Exp}(1)$ lifetime. A planner observes the compatibility graph over time (in the informed variant, also learns when an agent is about to depart) and decides when and whom to match (a match removes two compatible present agents). The objective is to minimize the long-run fraction of agents who perish unmatched (or expected waiting plus loss). Akbarpour–Li–Oveis Gharan (2020) show greedy matching is near-optimal without departure information, while a 'patient' waiting policy is much better with it, and characterize the optimal waiting threshold within a restricted policy class. Question: characterize the optimal dynamic matching policy and the exact achievable loss in the general model — beyond the restricted class and beyond the specific sparsity regime.
problems.known_results: Greedy loses an $O(1/d)$ fraction without departure info; with departure info, waiting reduces loss to $O(e^{-d}/d)$-type rates; the optimal policy is characterized only within a restricted class and a specific random-graph regime (Akbarpour–Li–Oveis Gharan, JPE 2020). The general optimal policy, exact loss, and behavior on structured (non-random) compatibility graphs are open and actively studied.

where_posed.text: Restricted-class optimality and the general open question are in Akbarpour, Li, Oveis Gharan, 'Thickness and Information in Dynamic Matching Markets' (J. Political Economy 2020).

source.authors: Mohammad Akbarpour, Shengwu Li, Shayan Oveis Gharan
source.title: Thickness and Information in Dynamic Matching Markets

problems.title: Greedy dynamic matching with heterogeneous departure rates
problems.short_statement: In dynamic matching with Poisson arrivals, exponential abandonment and rewards, does the LP-selected greedy policy of Arnosti and Simon always achieve the tight $1/2$ ratio against the omniscient benchmark without any homogeneous-departure-rate assumption?
problems.open_problem: $$ Equivalently, determine whether a well-chosen deterministic greedy policy always obtains the best possible $1/2$ competitive ratio against the omniscient benchmark in the general dynamic matching-with-abandonment model.
problems.full_statement: There is a finite set of types $T$. Type $i$ agents arrive according to an independent Poisson process with rate $\lambda_i$, abandon if unmatched after an independent exponential time with rate $\mu_i$, and matching an earlier type-$i$ agent to an arriving type-$j$ agent earns reward $r_{ij}$. The objective is the long-run steady-state reward rate. The benchmark $\mathrm{OMN}(I)$ is an omniscient policy that knows the full arrival and departure sequence. A greedy policy fixes, for each arriving type, an ordered list of acceptable waiting types and immediately matches to the best present acceptable type if one exists. Arnosti and Simon define an LP-based procedure that selects a greedy policy $\mathrm{ALG}(I)$. They prove $V(\mathrm{ALG}(I),I) \ge \frac12\mathrm{OMN}(I)$ when all departure rates are equal, and in bipartite instances when departure rates are equal within each side; they also prove the two-type heterogeneous case. The open problem is to prove or disprove the same guarantee for arbitrary heterogeneous departure rates $\mu_i$ and rewards $r_{ij}$: $$ V(\mathrm{ALG}(I),I) \ge \frac12\mathrm{OMN}(I) \quad \text{for every instance } I. $$ Equivalently, determine whether a well-chosen deterministic greedy policy always obtains the best possible $1/2$ competitive ratio against the omniscient benchmark in the general dynamic matching-with-abandonment model.
problems.known_results: Collina et al. (2020) gave a $1/8$ guarantee against the omniscient benchmark in the general heterogeneous-arrival/departure model. Arnosti and Simon prove a tight $1/2$ guarantee for their LP-selected greedy policy under homogeneous departures, and for bipartite instances with same-side homogeneous departures; no online policy can guarantee more than $1/2$ even in homogeneous-departure instances. Their simulations find no counterexample in heterogeneous instances, and they prove the conjecture for two types.

where_posed.text: Conjecture 1 and the discussion section of Arnosti and Simon, 'Greedy Dynamic Matching': extend the $1/2$ omniscient-benchmark guarantee for the LP-selected greedy policy to general departure rates $\mu_i$.

problems.title: Optimal dynamic policy for kidney exchange with long cycles and chains
problems.short_statement: In a dynamically arriving kidney-exchange pool, is matching immediately (greedily) optimal once exchanges via cycles of length $\ge 3$ or altruistic-donor chains are allowed - and if not, what is the optimal or best competitive policy?
problems.open_problem: determine the optimal (or best competitive) dynamic policy when cycles of length $\ge 3$ and/or chains are allowed in general graphs, and quantify when batching/waiting strictly beats greedy.
problems.full_statement: Incompatible patient–donor pairs arrive over time to an exchange pool; a directed compatibility graph forms among present pairs. A transplant is realized by clearing a directed cycle of length at most $L$ (e.g. $L=2$ pairwise swaps or $L=3$ three-way exchanges) or a chain initiated by a non-directed (altruistic) donor; cleared pairs leave. Pairs may also depart/perish if unmatched. The planner chooses when to match and which cycles/chains to clear, to maximize the number (or weighted value) of transplants, or minimize average waiting. Anderson–Ashlagi–Gamarnik–Kanoria (2017) show in a random-graph dynamic model that, for $L=2$, greedy (immediate) matching minimizes average waiting, and for $L=3$ greedy is optimal within a broad class. Question: determine the optimal (or best competitive) dynamic policy when cycles of length $\ge 3$ and/or chains are allowed in general graphs, and quantify when batching/waiting strictly beats greedy.
problems.known_results: For 2-way exchange, greedy minimizes waiting (Ünver 2010; Anderson et al. 2017). For 3-way, greedy is optimal within a broad policy class. With longer cycles, chains, heterogeneous types, or general compatibility structure, the optimal policy and its competitive ratio are open; recent work studies cost-balancing and 'when to match' principles.

where_posed.text: Open beyond the cases settled in Anderson, Ashlagi, Gamarnik, Kanoria, 'Efficient Dynamic Barter Exchange' (Operations Research 2017; SODA 2015).

problems.title: Cost of the greedy algorithm for dynamic spatial matching
problems.short_statement: Demand and supply points arrive i.i.d. uniform in $[0,1]^d$ and each demand is matched online to an available supply, minimizing expected average matched distance. The minimum achievable cost is known across dimensions; what is the expected cost of the natural greedy rule (match each arrival to its nearest available counterpart)?
problems.open_problem: Open problem: characterize the expected average matching cost of the \emph{standard greedy} algorithm - match each arriving demand unit to the nearest currently available supply unit (and vice versa in the fully dynamic model) - in the semi-dynamic and fully dynamic models, for each dimension $d$.
problems.full_statement: Supply and demand units are located i.i.d. uniformly in $[0,1]^d$. Consider two online models: the \emph{semi-dynamic} model (a pool of $N$ supply units is in place and $N$ demand units arrive sequentially, each matched on arrival to a still-available supply unit), and the \emph{fully dynamic} model (both supply and demand units arrive over time and unmatched units wait in the system). The cost is the expected average Euclidean distance between matched pairs as a function of system size $N$ (and any excess supply $M$). The nearest-neighbor distance $\Theta(N^{-1/d})$ is a lower bound. Kanoria (2021) determines the \emph{minimum achievable} cost in essentially all regimes: $\Theta(N^{-1/2})$ for $d=1$, $\Theta(\sqrt{\log N}\,/\sqrt{N})$ for $d=2$, and $\Theta(N^{-1/d})$ for $d\ge 3$ (static / semi-dynamic), achieved by a Hierarchical Greedy algorithm. \textbf{Open problem:} characterize the expected average matching cost of the \emph{standard greedy} algorithm - match each arriving demand unit to the nearest currently available supply unit (and vice versa in the fully dynamic model) - in the semi-dynamic and fully dynamic models, for each dimension $d$. Is standard greedy within a constant (or polylogarithmic) factor of the minimum achievable cost, or is it strictly (polynomially) worse?
problems.known_results: Kanoria (2021) gives tight bounds on the minimum achievable cost in essentially all regimes (via Hierarchical Greedy), and notes that 'a sharp analysis of the standard greedy algorithm is challenging even in the semi-dynamic setting, and analyzing greedy in the fully dynamic setting may be even harder.' For $d=1$ with excess supply $M=\epsilon N$, an $O(\log^3 N / N)$ upper bound on greedy's cost is known (Akbarpour, Alimohammadi, Li, Saberi 2021). The general behavior of standard greedy, especially in the fully dynamic model and for $d=2$, is open.

where_posed.text: Kanoria, 'Dynamic Spatial Matching' - the difficulty of analyzing standard greedy is stated in the model/results discussion (Sections 3-4) and the open directions (Section 7).

problems.title: Parameterized complexity of single-machine throughput scheduling with release dates
problems.short_statement: For single-machine throughput scheduling with release dates, are the unweighted and weighted tardy-job objectives W[1]-hard when parameterized by the maximum processing time?
problems.open_problem: Determine whether $1|r_j,p_j\le c|\sum U_j$ is polynomial-time solvable for each fixed processing-time bound $c$, and settle the weighted constant-$c$ case.
problems.full_statement: In $1|r_j|\sum U_j$ and $1|r_j|\sum w_jU_j$, each job $j$ has processing time $p_j$, release date $r_j$, due date $d_j$, and in the weighted case weight $w_j$. A nonpreemptive single-machine schedule chooses start times $S_j\ge r_j$ with no overlap; $U_j=1$ iff $C_j=S_j+p_j>d_j$. The objective is to minimize the number, or total weight, of tardy jobs. Let $p_{\max}=\max_j p_j$. The open problem asks whether these problems are W[1]-hard parameterized by $p_{\max}$, or instead fixed-parameter tractable (or even in XP) for this parameter. A positive FPT result would move toward polynomial-time algorithms for bounded-processing-time throughput scheduling; a hardness result would explain why this regime resists classical algorithms.
problems.known_results: The special case without release dates, $1||\sum U_j$, is polynomial-time solvable, and $1||\sum w_jU_j$ is weakly NP-hard but pseudo-polynomial. Sgall asked whether $1|r_j,p_j\le c|\sum U_j$ is polynomial-time solvable for constant $c$ and whether the weighted case is NP-hard for constant $c$. Mnich and van Bevern formulate the parameterized question and note that even membership in XP was open at publication.

where_posed.text: Open Problem 3 in Mnich and van Bevern, 'Parameterized complexity of machine scheduling: 15 open problems' (Computers & Operations Research 2018).

problems.title: FPT approximation scheme for job-shop makespan parameterized only by machines
problems.short_statement: Does job-shop makespan minimization admit a $(1+\epsilon)$-approximation running in $f(m,\epsilon)\operatorname{poly}(n)$ time, with dependence exponential only in the number of machines and accuracy?
problems.open_problem: Determine whether job-shop makespan admits an FPT approximation scheme parameterized only by the number of machines and $\epsilon$, without dependence on maximum operations per job.
problems.full_statement: In $J||C_{\max}$, each job consists of an ordered list of operations, where each operation must be processed nonpreemptively on a specified machine for a specified duration; each machine processes at most one operation at a time and operations of a job must respect their order. The objective is to minimize the makespan $C_{\max}$. For a fixed number $m$ of machines, job-shop scheduling admits PTAS/FPT-AS-type results when additional structural parameters are used, for example the maximum number $n_{\max}$ of operations per job. The open problem asks whether there is an algorithm that, for every $\epsilon>0$, returns a $(1+\epsilon)$-approximation for $J||C_{\max}$ in time $f(m,\epsilon)\cdot \operatorname{poly}(n)$, without dependence on $n_{\max}$ or other job-structure parameters outside the polynomial factor.
problems.known_results: Open-shop and flow-shop makespan have fixed-parameter approximation schemes parameterized by $m$ and $\epsilon$ in classical work. For job shop, Jansen, Solis-Oba, and Sviridenko obtain a $(1+\epsilon)$-approximation with dependence on $m$, $n_{\max}$, and $\epsilon$. Mnich and van Bevern list removing $n_{\max}$ from the parameter dependence as Open Problem 13.

problems.title: Open-shop scheduling with sequence-dependent batch setup times
problems.short_statement: Is unit-time open-shop makespan scheduling with sequence-dependent batch setup times fixed-parameter tractable in the number of batches?
problems.open_problem: Determine whether batch/open-shop scheduling with setup times is fixed-parameter tractable in the number of batches alone.
problems.full_statement: In $O|p_{ij}=1,ST_{sd,b}|C_{\max}$, each job has one unit-time operation on each machine, jobs are partitioned into $g$ batches, and a machine incurs a sequence-dependent setup time $s_{pq}$ when it switches from processing a job of batch $p$ to a job of batch $q$. The processing order of a job's operations is unconstrained, each machine handles at most one operation at a time, and each job is processed by at most one machine at a time. The objective is to minimize makespan. The open problem asks whether this problem is fixed-parameter tractable parameterized by the number of batches $g$, ideally also under high-multiplicity encoding where the number of jobs in each batch is given compactly.
problems.known_results: The problem generalizes the traveling salesperson problem and is NP-hard already for one machine. Van Bevern and Pyatkin give algorithms for cases where every batch contains more jobs than machines, and polynomial algorithms when both the number of machines and number of batches are constants. Mnich and van Bevern list FPT in the number of batches alone as Open Problem 14.

problems.title: Instance-dependent complexity of fixed-budget best-arm identification
problems.short_statement: For pure-exploration multi-armed bandits with a fixed sampling budget, characterize the instance-dependent exponential error rate and identify an algorithm class that achieves it.
problems.open_problem: The open problem is to find the corresponding fixed-budget theory: does there exist a natural algorithm class $\mathcal{A}$ and a function $\Gamma^*_{fb}(\mu)$ such that every algorithm in $\mathcal{A}$ satisfies $$\liminf_{n\to\infty}\frac{n}{\log(1/p_{\mu,n})}\ge \Gamma^*_{fb}(\mu),$$ and some algorithm in $\mathcal{A}$ satisfies the matching upper bound for every instance $\mu$?
problems.full_statement: Consider a finite-armed bandit instance $\mu=(\mu_1,\dots,\mu_k)$ with a unique best arm $I^*(\mu)=\arg\max_i \theta_i$, where $\theta_i$ is the mean of arm $i$. In the fixed-budget best-arm identification setting, an algorithm adaptively chooses arms for exactly $n$ samples and then outputs an estimate $\hat I_n$ of $I^*(\mu)$. Let $p_{\mu,n}=\mathbb{P}_{\mu}(\hat I_n\ne I^*(\mu))$. The fixed-confidence setting has a sharp asymptotic complexity characterized by a max-min KL expression and Track-and-Stop algorithms. The open problem is to find the corresponding fixed-budget theory: does there exist a natural algorithm class $\mathcal{A}$ and a function $\Gamma^*_{fb}(\mu)$ such that every algorithm in $\mathcal{A}$ satisfies $$\liminf_{n\to\infty}\frac{n}{\log(1/p_{\mu,n})}\ge \Gamma^*_{fb}(\mu),$$ and some algorithm in $\mathcal{A}$ satisfies the matching upper bound for every instance $\mu$? Relatedly, is there an adaptive algorithm other than uniform sampling that is uniformly no worse than uniform sampling in this fixed-budget error-exponent sense?
problems.known_results: The fixed-confidence setting is characterized by Chernoff-style lower bounds and Garivier-Kaufmann Track-and-Stop algorithms. For fixed-budget best-arm identification, non-adaptive allocation has a Glynn-Juneja large-deviations characterization, but the optimal adaptive instance-dependent complexity is not known. Two natural conjectures equating the fixed-budget complexity with fixed-confidence or non-adaptive complexities fail in general, though special two-arm Gaussian cases are understood. The open problem is to identify the correct adaptive instance-dependent complexity for fixed-budget BAI, or to prove that no clean analogue of the fixed-confidence characterization exists.

where_posed.text: Open Problems 1 and 2 in Qin, 'Open Problem: Optimal Best Arm Identification with Fixed Budget' (COLT open problem note).

problems.title: Best competitive ratio for Adwords with unknown budgets
problems.short_statement: In the Adwords problem where advertiser budgets are unknown until exceeded, what is the optimal randomized competitive ratio?
problems.open_problem: The open problem is to determine the best possible randomized competitive ratio for Adwords with unknown budgets, and whether a budget-oblivious or different randomized algorithm achieves it.
problems.full_statement: In the Adwords problem, queries arrive online. Each query $t$ has bids $b_{it}$ from advertisers $i$, and assigning $t$ to $i$ earns $b_{it}$ up to advertiser $i$'s remaining budget $B_i$. In the classical model, all budgets $B_i$ are known to the algorithm. In the unknown-budget model, bids are known on arrival but $B_i$ is not known in advance; the algorithm only learns that an advertiser's budget has been exceeded when an attempted allocation fails or exhausts it. Decisions are irrevocable, and the benchmark is the offline optimum that knows the entire arrival sequence and all budgets. Greedy is $1/2$-competitive and no deterministic algorithm can do better. Udwani gives a randomized budget-oblivious generalized perturbed-greedy algorithm with competitive ratio at least $0.522$ and shows this algorithm is strictly below $1-1/e$. The open problem is to determine the best possible randomized competitive ratio for Adwords with unknown budgets, and whether a budget-oblivious or different randomized algorithm achieves it.
problems.known_results: Known-budget Adwords admits the classic $1-1/e$ guarantee under small bids. With unknown budgets, deterministic algorithms are capped at $1/2$. Udwani's randomized GPG-style algorithm improves this to at least $0.522$, with an upper bound below $1-1/e$ for that algorithm. The exact optimal randomized ratio remains open.

where_posed.text: Conclusion of Udwani, 'Adwords with Unknown Budgets and Beyond', states the immediate open question of finding the best possible competitive ratio guarantee for Adwords with unknown budgets.

problems.title: Efficient robust policies for heterogeneous data-driven decisions
problems.short_statement: In the heterogeneity-ball framework for data-driven decisions, can one efficiently compute or approximate general robust policies that retain the known rate-optimal worst-case regret guarantees?
problems.open_problem: The open problem is algorithmic: can these general robust policies be computed efficiently, or approximated in a way that preserves their rate-optimal regret guarantees, for broad classes of OR/MS problems beyond the worked examples?
problems.full_statement: Consider the framework of Besbes, Ma, and Mouchtaki: a decision $x\in\mathcal{X}$ is evaluated on a future distribution $\mu$, while historical observations are independent but may come from different unknown distributions $\nu_1,\dots,\nu_n$ lying in a radius-$\epsilon$ heterogeneity ball around $\mu$ under a specified metric such as Kolmogorov, Wasserstein, total variation, or a divergence. A data-driven policy maps historical samples into a decision, and performance is measured by asymptotic worst-case expected regret relative to the oracle decision optimal for $\mu$. SAA is rate-optimal for some problem/metric pairs but fails badly for others, such as pricing under Wasserstein heterogeneity and ski-rental variants. The paper proposes robust alternatives such as RDRO and DRO policies and proves rate-optimality in several settings. The open problem is algorithmic: can these general robust policies be computed efficiently, or approximated in a way that preserves their rate-optimal regret guarantees, for broad classes of OR/MS problems beyond the worked examples?
problems.known_results: Besbes, Ma, and Mouchtaki give a formal heterogeneity-ball model, upper/lower bounds for SAA, and problem-dependent rate-optimal robust policies for examples including newsvendor, pricing, and ski rental. They explicitly leave open efficient computation of the RDRO/DRO policies, approximations that still perform well, and broader finite-sample/adaptive-to-heterogeneity guarantees.

where_posed.text: Section 6 of Besbes, Ma, and Mouchtaki, 'Beyond IID: data-driven decision-making in heterogeneous environments', leaves open efficient computation/approximation of robust policies that perform well under heterogeneity.

problems.title: Talagrand's decomposition conjecture for empirical processes
problems.short_statement: Does every bounded empirical process decompose into a non-cancellation part plus a generic-chaining part, as predicted by Talagrand's program?
problems.open_problem: Talagrand's conjecture asks whether these are the only mechanisms: can one always find a decomposition $\mathcal{F}\subset \mathcal{F}_1+\mathcal{F}_2$ such that $$\mathbb{E}\sup_{f_1\in\mathcal{F}_1}\sum_{i\le N}|f_1(X_i)|\le \sqrt N\,S_N(\mathcal{F}),$$ $$\gamma_2(\mathcal{F}_2,d_2)\le L S_N(\mathcal{F}),\qquad \gamma_1(\mathcal{F}_2,d_\infty)\le L\sqrt N\,S_N(\mathcal{F})$$ for a universal constant $L$?
problems.full_statement: Let $(X_i)_{i\le N}$ be i.i.d. random variables on a measurable space and let $\mathcal{F}$ be a countable class of measurable functions. Define the empirical-process supremum $$S_N(\mathcal{F})=\mathbb{E}\sup_{f\in\mathcal{F}}\left|\frac{1}{\sqrt N}\sum_{i\le N}(f(X_i)-\mathbb{E}f(X_i))\right|.$$ There are two basic mechanisms for controlling $S_N(\mathcal{F})$: a pointwise/no-cancellation bound through $\sum_i |f(X_i)|$, and a cancellation/chaining bound involving $\gamma_2(\mathcal{F},d_2)$ and $\gamma_1(\mathcal{F},d_\infty)$, where $d_p(f,g)=\|f-g\|_p$. Talagrand's conjecture asks whether these are the only mechanisms: can one always find a decomposition $\mathcal{F}\subset \mathcal{F}_1+\mathcal{F}_2$ such that $$\mathbb{E}\sup_{f_1\in\mathcal{F}_1}\sum_{i\le N}|f_1(X_i)|\le \sqrt N\,S_N(\mathcal{F}),$$ $$\gamma_2(\mathcal{F}_2,d_2)\le L S_N(\mathcal{F}),\qquad \gamma_1(\mathcal{F}_2,d_\infty)\le L\sqrt N\,S_N(\mathcal{F})$$ for a universal constant $L$? A related selector-process version asks for an analogous decomposition for Bernoulli selectors $\delta_i$.
problems.known_results: Talagrand's majorizing-measure theorem fully characterizes Gaussian process suprema via generic chaining. Bednorz and Latała solved the Bernoulli conjecture, showing that a Bernoulli process decomposes into an $\ell^1$ no-cancellation part and a Gaussian/chaining part. They identify this as one step in Talagrand's broader program and record the selector-process and empirical-process decomposition conjectures as open.

where_posed.text: Conjectures 9.1 and 9.2 in Bednorz and Latała, 'On the boundedness of Bernoulli processes', attributed to Talagrand's program and book.

problems.title: Efficient recovery at the random graph matching threshold
problems.short_statement: For correlated Erdos-Renyi graph matching, can polynomial-time algorithms reach the information-theoretic recovery thresholds?
problems.open_problem: Determine whether there is a polynomial-time algorithm for unseeded correlated Erdos-Renyi graph matching whose exact-recovery or almost-exact-recovery threshold matches the information-theoretic threshold, including the sparse regimes where current sharp results are only partly algorithmic.
problems.full_statement: Two observed graphs $G_1$ and $G_2$ are generated from a common parent Erdos-Renyi graph on $n$ vertices, followed by independent edge subsampling or an equivalent edge-correlation mechanism, and then the vertices of one graph are permuted by an unknown permutation $\pi$. The statistical task is to recover $\pi$ exactly, or to correctly match a positive or $1-o(1)$ fraction of vertices. Maximum likelihood gives the relevant information-theoretic benchmark but is not a polynomial-time algorithm. The open problem is to close the algorithmic gap: find a polynomial-time estimator that succeeds down to the information-theoretic threshold, or prove a rigorous computational barrier, especially for sparse graphs where local graph structure rather than dense spectral information drives the transition.
problems.known_results: Wu, Xu, and Yu determine sharp reconstruction thresholds for dense correlated random graph matching and determine sparse Erdos-Renyi thresholds up to constant factors; their positive results use a refined analysis of maximum likelihood. Seeded variants are much better understood algorithmically, but the remaining open part is the unseeded polynomial-time threshold: the information-theoretic characterization does not by itself give an efficient matching algorithm.

where_posed.text: This entry formulates the algorithmic gap suggested by the information-theoretic thresholds of Wu, Xu, and Yu and by the contrast with seeded graph-matching algorithms.

problems.title: Sharp computational thresholds for planted structures
problems.short_statement: Can the conjectured computational thresholds for planted dense subgraph and submatrix problems be made sharp under clean average-case assumptions?
problems.open_problem: Give sharp polynomial-time thresholds, or matching conditional lower bounds, for planted dense subgraph and submatrix localization across the sparse and growing-structure regimes where information-theoretic recovery is possible but no efficient algorithm is known.
problems.full_statement: A planted-structure problem starts with a random background object, such as an Erdos-Renyi graph or a noisy matrix, and then plants a structured signal, such as a denser vertex subset, a community, or an elevated submatrix. The information-theoretic threshold asks when the planted object can be detected or localized by any estimator, while the computational threshold asks when this can be done in polynomial time. The open problem is to turn the broad statistical-computational gap picture into sharp theorems for canonical planted dense subgraph and submatrix models: identify the exact efficient boundary, prove algorithms up to that boundary, and prove lower bounds beyond it under transparent assumptions such as planted clique hardness.
problems.known_results: Wu and Xu survey a general recipe for information-theoretic limits and planted-clique reductions, and earlier work by Hajek, Wu, and Xu gives computational lower bounds for community detection in random graphs. These results explain why an intermediate regime can exist. What remains open is the sharp boundary: in many planted dense subgraph and submatrix regimes the information-theoretic limit, best known efficient algorithm, and conditional lower bound still do not coincide.

where_posed.text: Wu and Xu frame planted-structure inference around separate information-theoretic and computational thresholds and describe planted-clique reductions as the standard route to conditional computational limits.

problems.title: The CTCS(infinity) limit and the beta(2,1) coalescent
problems.short_statement: What is the precise relationship between the infinite critical beta-splitting tree and the beta(2,1) coalescent?
problems.open_problem: Characterize the limiting continuous-time critical beta-splitting tree CTCS($\infty$), and determine whether and how its exchangeable partition dynamics are represented by the beta(2,1) coalescent.
problems.full_statement: In the critical beta-splitting model, a clade of size $m$ is split into subclades of sizes $i$ and $m-i$ with probabilities proportional to $1/(i(m-i))$. Aldous and Janson construct a consistent family of continuous-time trees CTCS($n$), $n\ge 2$, under the operation of deleting a random leaf and pruning. Consistency suggests an infinite exchangeable limit CTCS($\infty$). The open problem is to give a complete probabilistic description of this infinite object and to explain its relation to the beta(2,1) coalescent: whether the same merger structure appears exactly, after a time change, in a marginal projection, or only through an asymptotic correspondence.
problems.known_results: Aldous and Janson give an overview of the critical beta-splitting model, including a central limit theorem for leaf heights, a description of the fringe distribution seen from a random leaf, and a canonical embedding into the CTCS($n$) continuous-time model. The remaining open part is structural: the infinite exchangeable limit is only partly understood, and the connection to the beta(2,1) coalescent is explicitly identified as a major unresolved issue.

where_posed.text: The abstract and overview of Aldous and Janson, 'The Critical Beta-splitting Random Tree II: Overview and Open Problems', state that many open problems remain, especially the relation between CTCS(infinity) and the beta(2,1) coalescent.

source.title: The Critical Beta-splitting Random Tree II: Overview and Open Problems

problems.title: Sharp cover times for random growing balls
problems.short_statement: For random growing balls on compact metric spaces, can one obtain sharp cover-time asymptotics beyond weak concentration?
problems.open_problem: Characterize the cover-time distribution for sequentially arriving randomly centered growing balls on broad classes of compact metric spaces, including sharp asymptotics, constants, and geometric conditions for concentration or limiting laws.
problems.full_statement: Let $(S,d)$ be a compact metric space with a probability measure $\mu$. Centers $X_1,X_2,\dots$ are sampled from $\mu$, and the ball born at time $i$ grows deterministically with age, so at time $t$ it covers a set of the form $B(X_i,r(t-i))$. The cover time is the first $t$ at which the union of all active growing balls covers $S$. Aldous proves general weak-concentration bounds and records basic results for this model. The open problem is to go beyond existence-type bounds: identify the leading term, second-order correction, limiting distribution, and dependence on metric entropy, local dimension, boundary effects, and irregular geometry.
problems.known_results: Aldous gives weak-concentration results for coverage by fixed-radius random sets and for randomly centered growing balls, using general bounds for arbitrary random sets and monotone hitting times. The remaining open part is the sharp probabilistic theory of the growing-ball model: weak concentration does not determine the exact cover-time scale, constants, or limiting laws for natural compact spaces.

where_posed.text: Aldous, 'Covering a compact space by fixed-radius or growing random balls', records basic results and open problems for the growing-ball random coverage model.

problems.title: Oracle-efficient agnostic contextual bandits
problems.short_statement: Can the optimal oracle-efficient contextual bandit reduction be extended from realizable models to agnostic or misspecified models?
problems.open_problem: Design an oracle-efficient contextual bandit algorithm for general agnostic or misspecified function classes that matches the best possible regret guarantees using only a small number of calls to standard offline learning or regression oracles.
problems.full_statement: In stochastic contextual bandits, a learner observes a context, chooses an action, and receives only the reward of the chosen action. Under realizability, the conditional mean reward belongs to a known function class $\mathcal{F}$, and Simchi-Levi and Xu give a statistically optimal reduction to offline regression using only logarithmically many oracle calls. The open problem is to obtain an equally clean and practical reduction when the model is agnostic or misspecified: the best function in $\mathcal{F}$ may only approximate the true conditional rewards. The target is an algorithm whose regret scales with the right approximation and complexity terms, remains computationally efficient for general classes, and avoids substantially stronger oracle assumptions.
problems.known_results: Simchi-Levi and Xu solve the realizable version, giving an optimal and oracle-efficient contextual bandit algorithm. Follow-up work develops instance-dependent complexity measures for contextual bandits and reinforcement learning. The remaining open part is the agnostic reduction: once realizability is removed, the known clean oracle-efficient guarantee does not directly apply, and the optimal computational/statistical tradeoff is still not as settled as in the realizable case.

where_posed.text: This entry is the natural agnostic extension of the open problem solved by Simchi-Levi and Xu for the realizable setting.

problems.title: Limited-switch contextual bandits with knapsacks
problems.short_statement: What are the optimal regret rates for contextual bandits with knapsack constraints when the policy is allowed only a limited number of switches?
problems.open_problem: Characterize the minimax regret of contextual bandits with knapsacks under a switching budget, and design efficient algorithms whose regret has the correct dependence on horizon, resource budgets, number of resources, policy complexity, and number of allowed switches.
problems.full_statement: Bandits with knapsacks model online decisions that earn stochastic rewards while consuming limited resources. Contextual versions allow the reward and consumption distributions to depend on observed covariates and compete with a class of context-dependent policies. Limited-switch models add a practical constraint: the decision rule can be changed only a bounded number of times. Simchi-Levi, Xu, and Zhao solve a noncontextual limited-switch version for blind network revenue management and extend it to bandits with knapsacks. The open problem is to combine the two directions: obtain a sharp regret theory for contextual bandits with knapsacks under switching constraints, including whether the piecewise-constant dependence on the switch budget survives once policy classes and contexts enter the model.
problems.known_results: Simchi-Levi, Xu, and Zhao give matching upper and lower bounds for blind network revenue management and bandits with knapsacks under limited switches, showing that regret is governed by a piecewise-constant function of the switching budget and number of resources. Earlier work gives efficient algorithms for contextual bandits with knapsacks without limited switching. The remaining open part is the simultaneous contextual, resource-constrained, limited-switch model.

where_posed.text: This entry combines the limited-switch BwK question resolved by Simchi-Levi, Xu, and Zhao with the contextual BwK model of Agrawal, Devanur, and Li.
